opts: Because the shortage is inadvertent, Patricia has no reporting obligation; she may simply correct the ledger entry and move on.
An inadvertent trust shortage that results from a clerical error is not a true shortage and does not trigger any trust accounting obligations.

Patricia must immediately investigate and correct the shortage, replace the missing funds from personal funds if necessary to bring the trust account to the correct balance, and report the shortage to the LSO; the inadvertent nature of the error does not eliminate these obligations, although it may be relevant to the severity of the disciplinary consequences.

Patricia may correct the shortage by moving funds between client ledger accounts without replacing funds from her personal funds, since the total trust funds held are sufficient if properly allocated.
expl: A trust shortage exists whenever the trust bank account balance is less than the total of the individual client trust ledger balances — regardless of the cause. An inadvertent shortage arising from a clerical error is still a trust shortage triggering the same obligations as any other shortage: immediate investigation, remediation (including replacing funds from personal funds if necessary to restore the correct balance), and reporting to the LSO. The inadvertent nature of the error may be relevant to the LSO's assessment of the severity of the misconduct, but it does not eliminate any of the procedural obligations.
caseRef: LSO By-Law 9 (trust shortage — definition and obligations regardless of cause)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 | Trust Accounting and Financial Management

text:
Lawyer Orla acts for the purchaser in a residential real estate transaction. She holds the full purchase price of $820,000 in her trust account. The transaction fails to close because the vendor alleges the purchaser breached the agreement of purchase and sale. The purchaser instructs Orla to return the funds to them immediately. The vendor's lawyer writes to Orla demanding that the funds be paid to the vendor as liquidated damages for the purchaser's alleged breach. Orla believes the purchaser's position is correct, but the dispute is genuinely contested. The funds remain in trust.

What are Orla's trust accounting obligations with respect to the $820,000 held in trust, given the competing claims?
opts: Orla must follow her client's instructions and return the funds to the purchaser immediately; her duty is to act on client direction and the purchaser's claim to the funds is at least as strong as the vendor's.
Orla must pay the funds to whichever party she believes has the stronger legal claim, using her professional judgment, to prevent the funds from being frozen indefinitely in trust.

Orla must keep the funds in trust until the dispute is resolved; she cannot unilaterally disburse to her client or to the vendor's side; she must notify both parties of the competing claims, and if the parties cannot reach agreement, she should seek direction from the court or another appropriate mechanism such as interpleader.
Orla may transfer the funds to her general account to prevent liability while the dispute is pending, provided she maintains a ledger record of the amount owing to the prevailing party.
expl: When a lawyer holds funds in trust and there are competing claims to those funds — here, both the purchaser (return of deposit) and the vendor (liquidated damages) assert entitlement — the lawyer's obligation under LSO By-Law 9 and the trust accounting rules is to keep the funds in trust and not disburse to either side until the dispute is resolved. The fact that the funds belong to Orla's own client does not authorize her to pay them out on client instruction alone when a legitimate adverse claim exists. Disbursing unilaterally would expose Orla to liability for breach of trust accounting obligations and potentially to a claim by the party whose interest she prejudiced. The proper course is to: keep the funds in trust; notify both parties in writing; attempt to obtain an agreement; and if no agreement is reached, seek a court order (such as an interpleader application) to determine entitlement. The funds cannot be moved to a general account under any circumstances.
caseRef: LSO By-Law 9 (trust accounting — mixed and disputed funds); Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.2
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 | Trust Accounting and Financial Management

text:
Lawyer Mona receives a $10,000 advance from a client on Monday to be applied toward fees in an ongoing litigation matter. The fees have not yet been earned. Mona is busy and does not deposit the funds into her trust account until the following Thursday — four business days later.

Has Mona complied with her trust accounting obligations under By-Law 9 regarding the timing of deposit of client funds?
opts: Yes — a lawyer has a full business week to deposit client funds into trust.
No — By-Law 9 requires that funds received in trust be deposited to the mixed trust account no later than the close of business on the next banking day following receipt.
Yes — the deposit timing rules under By-Law 9 apply only to real estate transaction funds, not to litigation retainers.
No — By-Law 9 requires that all client funds be deposited to trust within three business days; Mona deposited on the fourth business day, which is late.
expl: By-Law 9 requires that funds received in trust be deposited to the lawyer's mixed trust account no later than the close of business on the next banking day after they are received. Mona received the funds on Monday but did not deposit until Thursday — four business days later — which clearly violates the prompt deposit requirement. The rule applies to all unearned client funds, not only real estate transactions.
caseRef: LSO By-Law 9 (Financial Transactions and Records of Licensees), s. 3
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 | Trust Accounting

text:
Lawyer Noel is acting for the purchaser in a commercial real estate transaction. The purchase price is $4,000,000. The seller insists that the deposit funds be held in a separate interest-bearing account exclusively for this transaction rather than in Noel's general mixed trust account. Noel is considering his options.

Which of the following accurately describes the use and purpose of a specific trust account under By-Law 9?
opts: A specific trust account is prohibited — all client funds must be pooled in a single mixed trust account.

A specific trust account is an account opened and maintained for the funds of a specific client or a specific transaction, separately from the mixed trust account; it is typically used where a client instructs the lawyer to hold funds in a separate interest-bearing account or where the nature of the transaction requires segregation of funds.
A specific trust account may only be opened where the Law Society of Ontario has approved it for the specific transaction.
A specific trust account operates identically to a mixed trust account and provides no separate identification of the funds belonging to a particular client.
expl: By-Law 9 contemplates both mixed trust accounts (where funds of multiple clients are pooled) and specific trust accounts (also called designated trust accounts) where funds belonging to a specific client or transaction are held separately. A specific trust account is appropriate for large transactions or where the client requests or requires that funds be held in a separate interest-bearing account. The interest earned in a specific trust account belongs to the client.
caseRef: LSO By-Law 9, s. 4 (specific trust accounts)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 | Trust Accounting

text:
Lawyer Oliver has been running a busy real estate practice for two years. He maintains a mixed trust account and keeps a trust ledger, a client trust listing, and receives monthly bank statements. Oliver reconciles his bank statement to his trust ledger on an ad hoc basis — roughly every three or four months — when he has time. He believes this frequency is adequate because he has never had a shortfall.

Has Oliver satisfied his trust reconciliation obligations under By-Law 9?
opts: Yes — trust reconciliation is required only annually for lawyers with a real estate practice.

No — By-Law 9 requires that a lawyer perform a three-way reconciliation of the trust bank statement, the trust ledger, and the client trust listing on a monthly basis; Oliver's ad hoc reconciliation every three to four months is non-compliant regardless of whether a shortfall exists.
Yes — the absence of a shortfall demonstrates that the accounts are in order, which satisfies the reconciliation requirement.
No — By-Law 9 requires reconciliation within 10 business days after each transaction, not on a monthly basis.
expl: By-Law 9 requires that a lawyer perform a three-way reconciliation at least monthly: the trust bank statement balance must be reconciled to the trust ledger balance, which must in turn reconcile to the total of the client trust listing (the sum of individual client balances). Monthly reconciliation is a mandatory compliance obligation, not a best practice. The absence of a known shortfall does not substitute for performing the reconciliation — discrepancies may exist without the lawyer being aware of them.
caseRef: LSO By-Law 9, s. 5 (trust reconciliation — monthly)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 | Trust Accounting

text:
Lawyer Petra has completed a real estate transaction and holds $8,500 in trust that represents funds owed to her for fees and disbursements. She has rendered a final account to the client. Rather than waiting for the client to provide written direction, Petra transfers the $8,500 from trust to her general account because she has already rendered the final account and believes the amount is undisputed.

Has Petra complied with the requirements for withdrawing funds from trust under By-Law 9?
opts: Yes — once a final account has been rendered, the lawyer is entitled to transfer trust funds to the general account without any further formality.
No — trust funds may only be withdrawn by a lawyer if the client first provides a written direction authorizing the transfer; rendering a final account alone does not authorize withdrawal.
Yes — rendering a final account is the functional equivalent of a written direction for trust withdrawal purposes.
No — trust funds may never be transferred to a lawyer's general account for fee payment; fees can only be paid in cash by the client.
expl: By-Law 9 establishes strict requirements for withdrawing funds from trust. A lawyer may withdraw funds from trust only in accordance with certain permitted grounds, which include having a written direction from the client, a court order, or other authorization. Rendering a final account creates an entitlement to be paid but does not itself constitute authorization to take money from trust. Petra must have a written direction from the client or another valid basis before transferring trust funds to her general account.
caseRef: LSO By-Law 9, s. 9 (withdrawals from trust)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 | Trust Accounting

text:
Lawyer Quinn closed her practice four years ago. She retained all her client files and trust records for three years after closing and then destroyed them to free up storage space. A former client now contacts her requesting copies of trust records related to a real estate transaction from five years ago. Quinn informs the client that all records have been destroyed.

Has Quinn satisfied her trust record-keeping retention obligations under By-Law 9?
opts: Yes — a three-year retention period for trust records is adequate under By-Law 9.
Yes — lawyers who close their practice are exempt from ongoing record-keeping obligations.

No — By-Law 9 requires that trust records, including books of account and supporting documentation, be retained for a minimum of six years; Quinn destroyed records after three years and failed to meet this requirement.
No — trust records must be retained permanently and may never be destroyed.
expl: By-Law 9 requires that lawyers retain their books of account, including trust records, for a minimum of six years from the end of the fiscal year to which they relate. Quinn retained records for only three years before destroying them, which does not satisfy the six-year minimum. The obligation to retain records for six years applies even after a lawyer closes their practice. The records from a transaction five years ago should still have been available.
caseRef: LSO By-Law 9, s. 18 (record retention — six years)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 | Trust Accounting

text:
Lawyer Tariq holds $12,000 in trust for a client whose commercial lease negotiation has concluded. Tariq has done the work and intends to charge $8,000 but has not yet prepared a formal invoice. He needs cash immediately and transfers $8,000 from the client's trust account to his general account, planning to send the invoice to the client later that week.

Has Tariq complied with By-Law 9's requirements for withdrawing fees from trust?
opts: Yes — the work is done and the entitlement to fees exists; Tariq may transfer the funds and send the invoice afterward.
No — By-Law 9 requires that a lawyer deliver an account (invoice) to the client before or at the same time as withdrawing fees from trust; withdrawing fees before billing is not permitted.
Yes — because Tariq intends to send the invoice that same week, the timing is close enough to satisfy By-Law 9.
No — but only because Tariq did not get a written direction from the client; if he had a written direction, taking fees before billing would be permissible.
expl: By-Law 9 requires that a lawyer deliver an account (invoice) to the client before withdrawing fees from trust. The entitlement to be paid — based on having completed the work — does not authorize a lawyer to take money from trust before billing. The account must be rendered first, so that the client has notice of and an opportunity to review the fees being charged before the funds leave trust. The written direction from the client (if required) also presupposes that the client has seen an account. Tariq's transfer of funds before issuing an invoice violates By-Law 9.
caseRef: LSO By-Law 9 (trust withdrawal — account must be delivered before withdrawal)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 | Trust Accounting and Financial Management

text:
Lawyer Sophia is acting for a plaintiff in a wrongful dismissal action. She holds $5,000 in trust for the client. An expert report is required and the expert invoices $3,000, payable directly to the expert. Sophia wants to pay the expert directly from the trust account on the client's behalf.

Under By-Law 9, may Sophia pay the expert directly from the trust account for this disbursement?
opts: No — all trust funds must be paid out exclusively to the client; Sophia must first transfer funds to her general account and then pay the expert from there.

Yes — a lawyer may withdraw funds from trust to pay legitimate third-party disbursements incurred on behalf of the client, provided the client has authorized the payment and the disbursement is documented; paying the expert directly from trust on the client's behalf is a permitted withdrawal.
No — expert fees are disbursements that the client must pay personally; they cannot be paid from client trust funds.
Yes — Sophia may pay any amount from trust to any third party at any time because trust funds belong to the client.
expl: By-Law 9 permits a lawyer to withdraw funds from trust to pay legitimate third-party disbursements incurred on behalf of the client, such as court filing fees, expert reports, or other authorized expenses. This is a recognized and common purpose for trust withdrawals. The key requirements are that the client has authorized the disbursement, the payment is properly documented in the trust ledger, and the withdrawal does not create or worsen a trust shortage. Paying the expert directly from trust is appropriate and does not require routing the funds through the general account.
caseRef: LSO By-Law 9 (trust withdrawal — permitted for authorized third-party disbursements)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 | Trust Accounting and Financial Management

text:
Lawyer Ingrid acts for a purchaser in a commercial real estate closing. She is instructed by email, purportedly from the vendor's lawyer, to wire $2,000,000 from trust to a new bank account number that differs from what was used in prior communications. Ingrid is pressed for time and, without verifying the instruction by phone, processes the wire transfer. The email was sent by a fraudster who had compromised the vendor's lawyer's email account. The funds are lost.

What additional safeguard should Ingrid have taken before processing the electronic trust transfer under By-Law 9 and professional standards?
opts: Ingrid had no further obligation — an email instruction from a lawyer is a valid and sufficient authorization for any trust wire transfer.
Ingrid should have obtained written direction from her own client before proceeding; the vendor's lawyer's email is not a relevant authorization for disbursing funds held in trust for the purchaser.

Before processing an electronic trust transfer — particularly where banking details have changed from prior communications — Ingrid was required to independently verify the instruction through a separate, confirmed communication channel (such as a phone call to a previously known number); failure to verify a changed wire transfer instruction is a recognized fraud vector that creates professional liability.
Ingrid's only obligation was to ensure the transfer instruction was in writing; since the email constituted a writing, she satisfied her requirements.
expl: Electronic trust wire transfers — especially those involving changed banking details — are a known vector for email compromise fraud. By-Law 9 requires that trust transfers be authorized and properly documented, but professional standards and best practices require lawyers to independently verify changes to wire transfer instructions through a confirmed, out-of-band communication channel (e.g., a phone call to a previously known and verified number). Relying solely on an email instruction — without verification — when the banking details have changed is professionally unreasonable and can result in personal liability for the lawyer if client funds are lost through fraud.
caseRef: LSO By-Law 9 (electronic trust transfers — authorization and safeguards); LSO Practice Advisories on wire transfer fraud
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 | Trust Accounting and Financial Management

text:
Lawyer Jerome is arguing a novel contract interpretation issue before the Ontario Superior Court. He is aware that the Ontario Court of Appeal issued a decision last year that, if applied, would be directly fatal to his argument. However, he also believes the Court of Appeal decision was wrongly decided and plans to argue that it should be distinguished or not followed. Jerome decides not to mention the Court of Appeal decision at all and instead waits to see if opposing counsel raises it.

Is Jerome's decision not to disclose the contrary Court of Appeal decision consistent with his obligations under Rule 5.1-2?
opts: Yes — Jerome has no obligation to disclose a decision he believes was wrongly decided; he can reserve his arguments for any decision opposing counsel raises.
Yes — the duty to disclose adverse authority only applies when the lawyer agrees the authority is correct and binding; if Jerome plans to argue it should not be followed, he need not volunteer it.

No — Rule 5.1-2 requires a lawyer as advocate to disclose to the tribunal any directly adverse controlling authority, even if the lawyer intends to argue it was wrongly decided or should be distinguished; Jerome must bring the Court of Appeal decision to the court's attention and may then make his arguments for why it should not be applied.
No — but Jerome must disclose the decision only if the opposing party discovers it and specifically asks Jerome to address it.
expl: Rule 5.1-2 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct requires a lawyer to disclose to the tribunal any directly adverse controlling authority known to the lawyer, regardless of whether the lawyer intends to argue the authority was wrongly decided. The obligation to disclose is separate from the obligation to follow — Jerome may vigorously argue the Court of Appeal decision should be distinguished or not followed, but he cannot simply conceal it from the court. Candour requires bringing all material controlling authority to the tribunal's attention so the court can make an informed decision.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.1-2 (adverse controlling authority — mandatory disclosure)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 | Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties

text:
Lawyer Yasmine is representing a landlord in an application before the Landlord and Tenant Board. She is aware of a binding provincial court decision that directly contradicts the statutory interpretation she is advancing, which was decided just three weeks earlier. Opposing counsel — a self-represented tenant — has not cited this decision and appears unaware of it. Yasmine decides not to mention it.

Does Yasmine have an obligation to disclose the contrary binding decision to the Landlord and Tenant Board even though opposing counsel has not raised it?
opts: No — in an adversarial system, a lawyer has no obligation to cite authorities that harm the client's case; discovering and citing helpful authorities is the opposing party's responsibility.

Yes — Rule 5.1-2 requires a lawyer as advocate to disclose to the tribunal any binding legal authority directly relevant to the matter that is adverse to the client's position, regardless of whether the opposing party has found it; the self-represented status of the tenant does not change this obligation.
No — the duty to disclose adverse legal authorities only applies in courts, not before administrative tribunals like the Landlord and Tenant Board.
No — the decision was issued only three weeks ago and is too recent to be considered binding; Yasmine has no obligation to cite newly released decisions.
expl: Rule 5.1-2 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct requires a lawyer as advocate to cite relevant binding legal authorities known to the lawyer, even if unfavourable to the client's position. This duty applies before all tribunals, including administrative bodies like the Landlord and Tenant Board. The fact that the opposing party is self-represented and has not found the authority does not relieve Yasmine of the obligation — the duty runs to the tribunal, not to the opposing party. A recently issued decision is binding if it meets the applicable binding authority standard.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.1-2 (disclosure of adverse binding authority)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 | Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties

text:
Lawyer Rosa is representing a defendant in a civil action. During her opening statement, she tells the judge that a particular witness is available to testify and will provide key evidence. Before the witness testifies, Rosa learns that the witness has left the country and will not be returning in time for trial. Rosa decides not to inform the court because she fears it will weaken her client's position and hopes the matter resolves before the issue arises.

Has Rosa breached her duty of candour to the tribunal under Rule 5.1-2?
opts: No — Rosa's opening statement was made in good faith at the time and she has no obligation to correct information that has since become inaccurate.
No — the duty of candour applies only to written submissions, not to oral statements made during trial.

Yes — Rule 5.1-2 prohibits a lawyer from knowingly making false statements to the tribunal and requires the lawyer to correct a false statement previously made; Rosa must inform the court that the witness will not be available.
No — strategic decision-making about what information to share with the court is within the scope of the advocate's discretion.
expl: Rule 5.1-2 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct imposes a duty of candour on lawyers appearing before tribunals. This duty includes not knowingly making false or misleading statements to the tribunal and, critically, correcting any false statement previously made to the court. Rosa initially represented to the judge that the witness would be available. Having learned this is no longer true, she is obligated to correct that representation. Allowing the court to proceed on a false basis violates her duty of candour and constitutes a breach of Rule 5.1-2.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.1-2
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 | Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties

text:
Lawyer Stephan is researching a motion for summary judgment. He finds a recent Ontario Court of Appeal decision that directly contradicts the position he is advancing for his client. He also finds an older Superior Court decision that supports his client's position. Stephan decides to cite only the Superior Court decision in his factum and omit any reference to the Court of Appeal decision, hoping opposing counsel will not find it.

Does Stephan's failure to cite the contrary Court of Appeal decision in his factum violate the Rules of Professional Conduct?
opts: No — a lawyer is under no obligation to assist the opposing party by citing authorities that harm the client's position; adversarial practice permits selective citation.
No — the duty to cite contrary authorities only arises if the opposing party has specifically requested disclosure of adverse case law.

Yes — Rule 5.1-2 requires a lawyer as advocate to cite any directly relevant authorities of which the lawyer is aware even if they are unfavourable to the lawyer's position; failure to disclose a directly contrary Court of Appeal decision misleads the court by omission.
No — the duty to cite contrary authority applies only in courts of final resort, not in motions before the Superior Court.
expl: Rule 5.1-2 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct provides that a lawyer, as advocate, must not deliberately fail to advise the tribunal of binding authorities that are directly relevant to the matter, even if they are unfavourable to the client's position. A recent Ontario Court of Appeal decision that directly contradicts the argument being made is exactly the type of authority that must be disclosed. Deliberately omitting it to mislead the court constitutes a breach of the duty of candour.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.1-2
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 | Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties

text:
Lawyer Priya brings an urgent ex parte motion seeking an injunction to freeze her client's business competitor's bank accounts. The competitor is not present or represented at the hearing. Priya is aware of two facts that weigh against granting the injunction: first, her client previously lost a similar motion on nearly identical facts six months ago; and second, the competitor has already commenced proceedings to challenge the underlying claim. Priya does not disclose either fact to the court.

What obligation does Rule 5.1-7 impose on Priya when bringing an ex parte application?
opts: Priya is not required to disclose adverse facts on an ex parte motion because the adversarial system allows each party to present only their strongest case.

On an ex parte application, a lawyer must make full and frank disclosure of all material facts known to the lawyer, including facts adverse to the client's position, because the opposing party is not present to correct the record; Priya's failure to disclose the prior failed motion and the competitor's proceedings violated this obligation.
Priya's duty of confidentiality prevents her from disclosing information about the prior motion because it concerns the client's litigation history.
The duty of full disclosure on ex parte applications applies only to criminal matters such as production order applications, not to civil injunction proceedings.
expl: Rule 5.1-7 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct imposes a heightened duty of candour on ex parte applications: because only one side is present, the lawyer bringing the motion must make full and frank disclosure of all material facts, including those adverse to the client's position. This is essential to ensure the court is not misled into granting relief based on an incomplete picture. The prior failed motion and the pending counter-proceedings are plainly material to the court's assessment of the injunction. Priya's failure to disclose this information is a breach of the heightened candour obligation in ex parte proceedings.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.1-7 (ex parte applications — full disclosure obligation)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 | Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties

text:
Lawyer Xavier is representing a plaintiff in a civil action. Frustrated by what he sees as excessive delays in scheduling a motion, he telephones the chambers of the presiding judge directly without notice to opposing counsel to request an urgent hearing date and to explain his concerns about the scheduling process. The judge's assistant takes a message. When opposing counsel later learns of the call, she objects.

Has Xavier engaged in improper ex parte communication with the court, and is the objection well-founded?
opts: No — contacting the court's scheduling office or chambers to request a hearing date is an administrative matter that is not subject to the prohibition on ex parte communications.

Yes — any direct communication with the court, including to scheduling staff or chambers, without notice to opposing counsel regarding the substance of the proceeding is improper ex parte communication; Xavier should have communicated through proper channels, such as a consent motion or scheduling conference notice to all parties.
No — ex parte communications with the court are only prohibited when they are made in writing; an oral telephone message does not violate any rule.
No — because Xavier did not actually speak with the judge, no ex parte communication occurred.
expl: The prohibition on ex parte communications with the court applies to any substantive communication with the court or judicial staff about a proceeding, without the knowledge of opposing counsel. Contacting chambers to complain about scheduling and request a hearing is not a purely administrative scheduling request — it involves conveying the lawyer's view of the scheduling process to judicial staff, which is a substantive communication about the matter. All parties are entitled to be present when any such communication is made. Xavier should have written to all parties and used the court's formal scheduling procedures to address his concerns.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.1-2 (ex parte communications with court)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 | Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties

text:
Lawyer Teresa is consulted by a client who wants to sue a neighbour for $500 for an alleged breach of a verbal agreement. The client acknowledges that the verbal agreement is not provable and that no loss was actually suffered — the client simply wants to use the lawsuit to harass the neighbour following a personal dispute. Teresa considers filing the claim.

What are Teresa's obligations under Rule 5.1-2 and the Rules of Professional Conduct regarding the proposed action?
opts: Teresa must file the claim because a lawyer is obligated to advance whatever position the client instructs.
Teresa must file the claim because the client has a right of access to courts and the merits of the claim are for a judge to assess.

Teresa must not commence proceedings that have no merit and are brought for an improper purpose; Rule 5.1-2(e) prohibits a lawyer from bringing proceedings that are without a reasonable basis and intended to abuse the court process.
Teresa may file the claim as long as she informs the client that the claim is unlikely to succeed.
expl: Rule 5.1-2(e) of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct provides that a lawyer must not knowingly assist or permit a client to do anything that the lawyer considers to be dishonest or dishonourable, and more specifically, must not institute proceedings that have no merit and are brought for an improper purpose. A lawsuit filed to harass rather than to vindicate a legitimate legal right is frivolous and an abuse of process. Teresa must decline to commence this action.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.1-2(e)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 | Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties

text:
Lawyer Winston is acting for a plaintiff in a motion for summary judgment. During oral argument, the judge asks Winston directly whether there is any evidence contradicting the plaintiff's version of events. Winston is aware of an email that seriously undermines the plaintiff's credibility on the key issue but that has not been produced by either party. Winston says 'no' to the judge's question.

Has Winston's answer to the judge's question breached his duty of candour under Rule 5.1-2?
opts: No — Winston is not required to disclose evidence harmful to his client's case; the adversarial system places that obligation on the opposing party.

Yes — when directly asked by the court whether there is contradictory evidence, a lawyer who is aware of such evidence cannot answer falsely; Rule 5.1-2 prohibits a lawyer from knowingly making a false statement to the tribunal, including by giving a false answer to a direct judicial inquiry.
No — Winston's obligation of confidentiality to his client prevents him from disclosing the email or acknowledging that it exists.
No — because the email has not been produced, it is not properly before the court and Winston has no duty to acknowledge its existence.
expl: Rule 5.1-2 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct prohibits a lawyer from knowingly making a false statement to a tribunal. When a judge directly asks whether contradictory evidence exists, a lawyer who is aware of such evidence cannot answer 'no' — that is a knowing false statement to the tribunal. The adversarial system does not authorize lying to a judge in response to a direct question. Winston was not required to volunteer the email unprompted, but once directly asked, he could not falsely deny knowledge of contradictory evidence.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.1-2 (duty not to mislead — false answer to direct judicial inquiry)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 | Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties

text:
Lawyer Marissa is representing a plaintiff in a personal injury action. In her written submissions, she accurately describes the medical evidence supporting the plaintiff's injuries but deliberately omits a subsequent medical report — prepared at her client's own request — that concluded the plaintiff had made a full recovery and had no ongoing impairment. Marissa reasons that the omitted report was unfavourable, that opposing counsel has a copy, and that candour does not require her to highlight evidence against her own client.

Has Marissa breached her duty of candour under Rule 5.1-2 by omitting the recovery report from her submissions?
opts: No — a lawyer has no obligation to highlight evidence that undermines her client's case; the adversarial system places the burden on opposing counsel to adduce contrary evidence.
No — because opposing counsel has the report, the court is not being misled; candour is only violated when the court has no access to the omitted information.

Yes — Rule 5.1-2 prohibits a lawyer from creating a false impression in the mind of the tribunal through selective presentation of facts; deliberately omitting a material medical report that contradicts the plaintiff's injury claim misleads the tribunal by omission, even if opposing counsel possesses the report.
No — the duty of candour applies only to statements of law, not to the selection of factual evidence presented in submissions.
expl: Rule 5.1-2 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct prohibits a lawyer from creating a false impression in the mind of the tribunal through selective presentation, omission of material facts, or misleading innuendo. Candour extends beyond avoiding outright lies — it encompasses not structuring submissions so as to convey a misleading picture of the evidence. The fact that opposing counsel has the report does not remedy the misleading impression created by Marissa's selective submissions. The court must be presented with a fair, not strategically distorted, account of the material facts.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.1-2 (misleading by omission — selective presentation of facts)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 | Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties

text:
Lawyer Valentina is defending an accused charged with assault. During trial preparation, the accused tells Valentina that the alibi witness Valentina intends to call will be lying — the accused was actually at the scene but claims his alibi witness has agreed to provide false testimony. The accused insists Valentina proceed and call the alibi witness anyway, since the Crown cannot disprove the alibi.

What must Valentina do under Rule 5.1-2 of the Rules of Professional Conduct?
opts: Valentina must call the alibi witness because the accused has the right to present a full defence and the Crown's burden to disprove the alibi is not affected by what Valentina knows privately.

Valentina must not call a witness she knows will give false testimony; she must advise the accused that she cannot present knowingly false evidence and that she must withdraw if the accused persists in the instruction; Valentina may not knowingly adduce perjured evidence regardless of the accused's instructions.
Valentina may call the witness because solicitor-client privilege protects her from having to act on information received from the accused during a privileged consultation.
Valentina must immediately disclose to the Crown that the alibi is false because her duty to the court overrides solicitor-client privilege in criminal proceedings.
expl: Rule 5.1-2 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct prohibits a lawyer from knowingly adducing false or perjured evidence before a tribunal. Once Valentina knows the intended alibi evidence is false, she cannot present it regardless of the accused's instructions. She must advise the accused that she cannot proceed on those instructions and that she will withdraw if he persists. Rule 5.1-2(f) makes clear that a lawyer must not, with knowledge that evidence is false, offer it. Valentina may not disclose the contents of the privileged communication to the Crown — but she must refuse to act on the instruction.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.1-2(f) (knowing adduction of perjured evidence)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 | Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties

text:
Lawyer Grant is preparing for a civil trial. His client will testify that a written invoice in the file is genuine and was signed by the other party. Grant has a nagging suspicion that the invoice may have been altered, but he has no proof — there are no forensic tests, no admissions, and no contradictory evidence. The client insists the invoice is authentic. Grant is trying to decide whether he can call his client to testify about the invoice.

Under Rule 5.1-2, may Grant call his client to testify about the invoice in these circumstances?
opts: No — even a suspicion that evidence may be false is enough to prevent a lawyer from adducing it; Rule 5.1-2 prohibits adducing any evidence the lawyer is not certain is truthful.

Yes — Rule 5.1-2 prohibits adducing evidence the lawyer knows to be false, not evidence the lawyer merely suspects may be false; Grant does not have knowledge of falsity and may call the evidence, but should remain alert.
No — Grant must conduct forensic testing before calling the client to testify; failing to verify the authenticity of documentary evidence violates Rule 5.1-2.
Yes — Grant may call the evidence because his duty of loyalty to his client requires him to advance the client's position on all available evidence regardless of any personal suspicion.
expl: Rule 5.1-2 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct prohibits a lawyer from adducing evidence the lawyer knows to be false. The critical word is 'knows' — the prohibition does not extend to evidence the lawyer merely suspects or has doubts about. If Grant has a suspicion but no actual knowledge that the invoice is false, he is not prohibited from calling his client to testify about it. The lawyer must act on actual knowledge, not unsubstantiated suspicion. Of course, if Grant later acquires actual knowledge that the invoice is a forgery, the analysis changes entirely.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.1-2 (presentation of evidence — knowing vs. suspecting evidence is false)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 | Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties

text:
Lawyer Sandra is acting for a defendant in a civil breach of contract action. During examinations for discovery, her client stated under oath that he had never signed the contract in dispute. Sandra relied on this in her written submissions. After the submissions were filed, the client admits to Sandra that he did in fact sign the contract — he had panicked during discovery and lied. Trial is three weeks away. The client instructs Sandra not to correct the record because doing so would destroy their case.

What must Sandra do upon learning that a false statement was made to the court in her submissions, based on her client's false discovery testimony?
opts: Sandra may continue without correction because her submissions were made in good faith based on her client's instructions at the time; she bears no responsibility for the client's false discovery evidence.

Sandra must take steps to correct the false record; Rule 5.1-2(i) requires a lawyer who has made a false statement to the court — even unknowingly — to take remedial steps once aware of the falsity, including correcting the submissions; if the client refuses, Sandra must withdraw.
Sandra may continue the trial without correction as long as she does not refer to the false discovery testimony again in her closing submissions.
Sandra must immediately disclose the client's admission to opposing counsel because her duty to the court overrides solicitor-client privilege in this context.
expl: Rule 5.1-2(i) of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct provides that a lawyer who has made a representation to the court that the lawyer later learns was false must take remedial steps to correct the false record. It does not matter that Sandra acted in good faith when she filed the submissions — once she learns of the falsity, she has an obligation to act. The appropriate steps are to advise the client that the record must be corrected and, if the client refuses, to withdraw from the representation. Sandra cannot continue building a case on what she now knows to be a false foundation. Importantly, she must not disclose the client's privileged admission to opposing counsel — the response is correction or withdrawal, not breach of privilege.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.1-2(i) (duty to rectify false statement to tribunal)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 | Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties

text:
Lawyer Ulrich is defending a commercial landlord in a wrongful eviction action. The tenant has strong evidence of the eviction. Ulrich's client instructs him to raise every possible defence, including defences that Ulrich knows have absolutely no legal or factual foundation, simply to delay the proceedings and impose maximum costs on the tenant. Ulrich proceeds as instructed.

Has Ulrich acted in accordance with his duties under the Rules of Professional Conduct?
opts: Yes — a defence lawyer's duty is to zealously advocate for the client by raising all possible defences; Ulrich is obligated to follow his client's instructions.
Yes — the court can assess costs against the landlord at the end of the proceedings, which is the appropriate mechanism to address unfounded defences.

No — Rule 5.1-2 prohibits a lawyer from asserting a position in proceedings where the lawyer knows the position is not supported by law and has no merit; using unfounded defences to delay proceedings and harass the opposing party is a breach of the advocate's duty.
Yes — as long as the unfounded defences are technically available at law, raising them is a legitimate tactical decision within the advocate's discretion.
expl: Rule 5.1-2 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct limits the advocate's role by prohibiting the assertion of positions in proceedings that the lawyer knows to be without merit. A lawyer cannot use litigation as a tool of delay and harassment by pleading defences that have no legal or factual foundation. Zealous advocacy does not extend to advancing knowingly baseless positions. The availability of costs as a remedy at the end of proceedings does not excuse the lawyer from complying with Rule 5.1-2 during the proceedings.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.1-2
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 | Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties

text:
Lawyer Frances represents an employee in a wrongful dismissal action against her former employer. The employer is represented by in-house legal counsel. Frances sends a letter directly to the employer's CEO — bypassing in-house counsel — to propose a settlement meeting, believing that the CEO has the ultimate authority to settle and that in-house counsel is obstructing resolution. In-house counsel objects to the direct contact.

Was Frances's direct letter to the CEO permissible under Rule 7.2-1 given that the employer is represented by in-house counsel?
opts: Yes — in-house counsel represents the corporation as an institution, not the individual employees who make up the corporation; the CEO is not a 'represented party' for the purposes of Rule 7.2-1.
Yes — where the lawyer believes that opposing counsel is obstructing settlement, direct contact with the represented party to propose settlement is a recognized exception to Rule 7.2-1.

No — Rule 7.2-1 prohibits direct contact with a person who is represented by counsel in the matter without the consent of that counsel; an employer represented by in-house counsel is a 'person represented by a lawyer' — the CEO, as a senior officer of the represented organization, falls within the scope of the rule; Frances's direct letter to the CEO violated Rule 7.2-1.
Yes — the rule against contacting represented parties only applies to individual persons, not corporations and their officers.
expl: Rule 7.2-1 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct prohibits a lawyer from communicating directly with a person known to be represented by another lawyer in the matter, without the other lawyer's knowledge and consent. When the opposing party is a corporation represented by in-house counsel, senior officers of the corporation — including the CEO — are covered by the rule because they speak for the corporation in the matter. Frances's belief that in-house counsel is obstructing settlement does not create an exception; she should have sought to resolve the issue through proper channels, such as a case conference or by raising the matter with the court.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-1 (no direct contact with represented party — including corporate officers)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 | Dealing with Opposing Parties and Unrepresented Persons

text:
Lawyer Arnav is representing a defendant in a civil harassment claim. During cross-examination of the plaintiff — a woman of South Asian origin — Arnav's questions repeatedly invoke the plaintiff's cultural background and make references to cultural attitudes toward women in ways that are irrelevant to the issues in the case. The plaintiff's counsel objects that the questions are discriminatory. Arnav argues that he is entitled to test the plaintiff's credibility and that any questions that might be adverse to a witness are permitted in cross-examination.

Do Arnav's questions violate the Rules of Professional Conduct?
opts: No — vigorous cross-examination is a fundamental right of a litigant, and lawyers may ask any questions they believe may affect credibility, including questions about cultural background.

Yes — Rule 6.3-1 of the Rules of Professional Conduct prohibits a lawyer from engaging in discriminatory conduct in the course of professional activities; using a witness's race or ethnic origin in an irrelevant and stereotyping manner during cross-examination constitutes discriminatory conduct in the practice of law.
No — Rule 6.3-1 only applies to conduct in the lawyer's office; it does not apply to conduct during courtroom proceedings.
No — the judge has the authority to control inappropriate questions in cross-examination, making the professional responsibility rules inapplicable to this situation.
expl: Rule 6.3-1 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct requires that a lawyer not discriminate on the basis of race, ancestry, place of origin, colour, ethnic origin, or similar grounds in the course of professional activities. The prohibition extends to conduct in court proceedings — it is not limited to the lawyer's office. Cross-examination that invokes a witness's ethnic or cultural background in a stereotyping or irrelevant manner constitutes discriminatory conduct in the practice of law. The advocate's right to test credibility is real but bounded by the obligation not to engage in discrimination.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 6.3-1 (anti-discrimination in professional activities)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 | Dealing with Opposing Parties and Unrepresented Persons

text:
Lawyer Petra is cross-examining a key plaintiff's witness in a civil fraud trial. The witness appears nervous. Petra speaks loudly, repeats questions that the witness has already answered, and makes sarcastic comments about the witness's memory. She also asks the witness if the witness is aware of 'what happens to people who lie in this courtroom' in an intimidating tone. When the plaintiff's counsel objects, Petra argues she is simply using vigorous cross-examination to expose inconsistencies.

Has Petra's conduct in cross-examination crossed into improper harassment of a witness?
opts: No — vigorous cross-examination necessarily involves pressure, repetition, and pointed questioning; all of Petra's conduct falls within the scope of legitimate cross-examination.

Yes — Rule 7.1 prohibits a lawyer from harassing or intimidating witnesses; while aggressive cross-examination to expose inconsistencies is proper, making veiled threats, sarcastic belittling, and repeating answered questions to exhaust and intimidate a witness crosses into prohibited harassment.
No — it is for the presiding judge, not the Rules of Professional Conduct, to control improper cross-examination; once the judge has not intervened, Petra's conduct is deemed appropriate.
No — the Rules of Professional Conduct do not apply to cross-examination tactics; advocacy decisions in the courtroom are exclusively governed by the court's own rules of procedure.
expl: Rule 7.1 and the Commentary to the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct prohibit a lawyer from harassing, intimidating, or abusing witnesses. There is a meaningful distinction between aggressive cross-examination — which is proper and often necessary — and harassment. Making veiled threats ('what happens to people who lie'), repetitive questioning of already-answered matters to wear down a witness, and sarcastic belittling are forms of witness intimidation and harassment that exceed the bounds of legitimate advocacy. The fact that the judge has not yet intervened does not make the conduct proper — professional responsibility obligations run independently of judicial intervention.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.1 (prohibition on harassment and intimidation of witnesses)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 | Dealing with Opposing Parties and Unrepresented Persons

text:
Lawyer Victor is acting for the plaintiff in a civil dispute. He learns that the opposing party, the defendant, is represented by a lawyer at a different firm. Victor wants to negotiate a settlement directly with the defendant, bypassing the defendant's lawyer, because he believes he can get better terms that way.

May Victor communicate directly with the defendant about the settlement without the knowledge or consent of the defendant's lawyer?
opts: Yes — direct client-to-client settlement discussions, even when mediated by lawyers, are a normal part of civil litigation.
Yes — Victor may communicate directly with the defendant to discuss settlement because settlement is in both parties' interests.
No — Rule 7.2-1 prohibits a lawyer from communicating directly with a person who is represented by another lawyer in the matter, except with the consent of that other lawyer or as authorized by law.
Yes — Victor may contact the defendant directly as long as he identifies himself as a lawyer and the defendant agrees to speak with him.
expl: Rule 7.2-1 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct provides that a lawyer must not communicate directly with a person who is represented by another lawyer in the matter, without the knowledge and consent of the other lawyer, or unless authorized by law. The rule protects represented parties from being approached directly by opposing counsel, which may result in the party making statements or agreements without the benefit of legal advice. Victor must communicate with the defendant through the defendant's lawyer.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-1
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 | Dealing with Opposing Parties and Unrepresented Persons

text:
Lawyer Wendy is acting for a landlord in a residential tenancy dispute before the Landlord and Tenant Board. The tenant is not represented by a lawyer and has stated they cannot afford one. Wendy approaches the tenant directly at the beginning of the hearing, explains the procedural issues, and negotiates terms for an agreement to end tenancy directly with the tenant without advising the tenant to get legal advice.

Has Wendy complied with the Rules of Professional Conduct in her dealings with the unrepresented tenant?
opts: Yes — lawyers are permitted to negotiate directly with unrepresented parties, particularly in administrative tribunals where legal representation is optional.

No — Rule 7.2-9 provides that when a lawyer deals with an unrepresented person, the lawyer must advise that person to obtain independent legal representation and must not take advantage of the person's lack of representation.
Yes — the fact that the tenant stated they cannot afford a lawyer means Wendy is permitted to deal directly without advising on legal counsel.
Yes — Wendy complied because she explained the procedural issues to the tenant, which sufficiently informed the tenant of their situation.
expl: Rule 7.2-9 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct requires that when a lawyer deals with an unrepresented person, the lawyer must urge the person to obtain independent legal advice and must not attempt to negotiate a resolution or take advantage of the person's lack of representation. Wendy's duty was to advise the tenant to get legal advice before negotiating terms of an agreement. Explaining procedural issues is not the same as advising the tenant to seek independent counsel, and negotiating directly without that advice may constitute taking advantage of the tenant's vulnerability.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-9
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 | Dealing with Opposing Parties and Unrepresented Persons

text:
Lawyer Xavier is retained to act as trial counsel for the plaintiff in a contract dispute. After examinations for discovery, it becomes clear that Xavier himself is a key witness on a crucial factual issue — he was present at the meeting where the alleged oral contract was made and has direct evidence about what was said. Xavier's client wants him to continue as trial counsel and also testify as a fact witness at trial.

What does Rule 5.2-1 of the Rules of Professional Conduct require in this situation?
opts: Xavier may act as both trial counsel and a fact witness because his evidence is helpful to his client's case.
Xavier must withdraw from acting as trial counsel if he is to testify as a witness; Rule 5.2-1 generally prohibits a lawyer from acting as both advocate and witness in the same proceeding.
Xavier may testify as a witness if his client provides written consent to both roles being performed by Xavier.
Xavier may testify as a witness on procedural matters only but must not testify on substantive factual issues while remaining as trial counsel.
expl: Rule 5.2-1 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct provides that a lawyer must not appear as advocate before a tribunal at a proceeding in which the lawyer is likely to be called as a witness. The roles of advocate and witness are incompatible because a witness's credibility is always at issue, and a lawyer who is both advocate and witness may confuse the trier of fact and impair the administration of justice. Xavier must withdraw as trial counsel and have a different lawyer take over if he is to testify.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.2-1
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 | Dealing with Opposing Parties and Unrepresented Persons

text:
Lawyer Quentin is acting for a plaintiff in a commercial dispute. He urgently needs to schedule a motion before the court and needs opposing counsel's cooperation to agree on a date. He learns that opposing counsel is on vacation for two weeks and has not arranged for another lawyer to cover the file. The opposing party — the defendant — is available and willing to discuss scheduling directly with Quentin. Quentin contacts the defendant directly to discuss scheduling the motion.

May Quentin contact the opposing party directly while opposing counsel is temporarily on vacation?
opts: Yes — because opposing counsel is unavailable, the practical necessity of the situation justifies direct contact with the represented opposing party to address the urgent scheduling matter.
Yes — scheduling is an administrative matter and does not fall within the prohibition on communicating with a represented party.

No — Rule 7.2-1 prohibits a lawyer from communicating directly with a represented party without the other lawyer's consent; the temporary unavailability of opposing counsel due to vacation does not create an exception; Quentin must attempt to reach opposing counsel or their firm, seek a coverage lawyer, or use the court's scheduling mechanisms.
Yes — the opposing party's willingness to speak with Quentin constitutes implied consent on behalf of their lawyer.
expl: Rule 7.2-1 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct prohibits a lawyer from communicating directly with a represented party without the other lawyer's knowledge and consent. There is no exception for temporary unavailability of opposing counsel due to vacation. The fact that scheduling is urgent does not create a practical-necessity exception. Quentin's options include: attempting to contact opposing counsel through the firm, requesting that the firm arrange coverage counsel, writing to opposing counsel's firm in the interim, or using the court's scheduling procedures (such as a scheduling conference or requisition). The opposing party's personal willingness to discuss matters does not substitute for their lawyer's consent.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-1 (no direct contact with represented party — counsel temporarily unavailable)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 | Dealing with Opposing Parties and Unrepresented Persons

text:
Lawyer Yul is acting for a commercial landlord. In his dealings with the tenant's lawyer, Yul frequently sends correspondence that is deliberately misleading about the state of negotiations, makes false representations about the landlord's settlement authority, and uses deliberately threatening language to intimidate opposing counsel. Yul justifies this by saying it is aggressive but effective advocacy.

Does Yul's conduct comply with the Rules of Professional Conduct regarding dealings with opposing counsel?
opts: Yes — lawyers are entitled to be aggressive advocates and there is no rule requiring courtesy or good faith toward opposing counsel.

No — Rule 7.1-1 requires a lawyer to be courteous and act in good faith in all dealings with other lawyers and parties; deliberately misleading opposing counsel, making false representations, and using threatening language constitutes sharp practice and professional misconduct.
Yes — aggressive negotiation tactics, including deliberate misrepresentation of settlement authority, are standard commercial practice accepted by the Rules.
No — but Yul's conduct is only problematic if opposing counsel formally objects; absent an objection, aggressive conduct is permissible.
expl: Rule 7.1-1 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct requires a lawyer to be courteous, civil, and act in good faith in all professional dealings. The Commentary to Rule 7.1-1 specifically addresses sharp practice — the use of tactics designed to mislead, surprise, or take unfair advantage of opposing counsel. Making false representations about settlement authority and deliberately misleading opposing counsel are prohibited. Yul's conduct constitutes sharp practice and a breach of the Rules regardless of whether it is 'effective.'
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.1-1
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 | Dealing with Opposing Parties and Unrepresented Persons

text:
Lawyer Zelda is acting for a client who alleges that a business associate defrauded her of $50,000 in a commercial dispute. Zelda sends a letter to the business associate — who is not represented by counsel — stating: 'Unless you pay my client $50,000 within 10 days, my client will instruct me to report you to the police for fraud and we will pursue the maximum criminal penalties available.' The civil claim has not yet been filed.

Does Zelda's letter violate Rule 7.2-7 of the Rules of Professional Conduct?
opts: No — a lawyer is entitled to inform an opposing party of the legal options available to the client, including criminal reporting, as part of legitimate settlement demands.

Yes — Rule 7.2-7 prohibits a lawyer from threatening to initiate criminal proceedings against a person primarily as a means to obtain a civil advantage; using the threat of a criminal complaint to coerce payment of a civil debt crosses the line from legitimate settlement demand to improper threat.
No — because the business associate is not represented by counsel, Zelda is entitled to be more direct in explaining the consequences of non-payment.
No — Rule 7.2-7 only applies after a civil action has been filed; pre-litigation threats are not covered by the rule.
expl: Rule 7.2-7 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct prohibits a lawyer from threatening to initiate criminal or regulatory proceedings against a person primarily as a device to extract a civil concession or payment. The letter Zelda sent explicitly conditions the withholding of a criminal complaint on payment of the civil debt, which is precisely the conduct the rule prohibits. This is distinct from legitimately advising a client on whether to report criminal conduct to the police — the prohibited conduct is using the threat as a settlement lever. The rule applies regardless of the merits of any potential criminal complaint and regardless of whether a civil action has been filed.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-7 (threatening criminal proceedings to gain civil advantage)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 | Dealing with Opposing Parties and Unrepresented Persons

text:
Lawyer Neil acts for a supplier in a payment dispute with a restaurant owner. Neil sends a demand letter stating: 'Unless your client pays the outstanding $18,000 within 15 days, my client will file a complaint with the Ontario Ministry of Labour reporting the underpayment of wages to your client's employees that we discovered during our business relationship, and will also report the suspected tax irregularities to the Canada Revenue Agency.' Neil's client has genuine information about both issues.

Does Neil's demand letter violate Rule 7.2-7 even though the threatened complaints involve regulatory matters, not criminal prosecution?
opts: No — Rule 7.2-7 applies only to threats of criminal proceedings; threatening regulatory or administrative complaints is permitted as a legitimate negotiation tactic.
No — because the underlying information about the labour and tax issues is genuine, Neil is entitled to inform the opposing party of his client's reporting options.

Yes — Rule 7.2-7 prohibits threatening to initiate criminal or regulatory proceedings against a person primarily as a means to obtain a civil advantage; conditioning the withholding of regulatory complaints on civil payment is equally prohibited whether the threatened proceedings are criminal or regulatory in nature.
Yes — but only if the restaurant owner has a lawyer; threats of regulatory proceedings against unrepresented parties are assessed under a different standard.
expl: Rule 7.2-7 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct expressly extends beyond criminal proceedings to include the threat to initiate regulatory proceedings as a lever to obtain civil advantage. The rule prohibits threatening 'to initiate or proceed with a criminal charge, or to make a complaint to a regulatory authority' primarily to gain a civil concession. The fact that Neil's client has genuine information about the violations does not authorize using those facts as bargaining chips conditioned on civil payment. The prohibited conduct is the conditioning of non-reporting on payment — not the reporting itself.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-7 (threatening criminal or regulatory proceedings to gain civil advantage)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 | Dealing with Opposing Parties and Unrepresented Persons

text:
Lawyer Harriet is acting for a vendor in the sale of a small business. The purchaser has no lawyer and tells Harriet he understands the deal and does not need one. Harriet is eager to close the transaction quickly and, without advising the purchaser to seek independent legal advice, presents him with the purchase agreement and asks him to sign immediately. The purchaser signs and later realizes the agreement contained terms significantly unfavourable to him.

Did Harriet comply with her obligations under Rule 7.2-9 when dealing with the unrepresented purchaser?
opts: Yes — the purchaser stated he understood the deal and did not need a lawyer; Harriet had no further obligation once the purchaser declined representation.

No — Rule 7.2-9 requires a lawyer dealing with an unrepresented party to advise that party to obtain independent legal advice before proceeding; the purchaser's self-assessed understanding of the deal does not relieve Harriet of this obligation.
Yes — the obligation to seek independent legal advice rests with the party, not the opposing lawyer; Harriet had no duty to recommend counsel to the other side.
Yes — Harriet complied because she presented the agreement in writing, which gives the purchaser all the information needed to make an informed decision.
expl: Rule 7.2-9 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct requires a lawyer who deals with an unrepresented person to urge that person to obtain independent legal advice before entering into a transaction or agreement. The purchaser's statement that he did not need a lawyer does not discharge Harriet's obligation — the duty is on the lawyer to make the recommendation, not to accept the unrepresented person's self-assessment as a substitute. Harriet should have clearly advised the purchaser to seek independent legal advice, confirmed this was understood, and allowed time for the purchaser to do so before proceeding to execution.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-9 (duty to advise unrepresented party to seek independent legal advice)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 | Dealing with Opposing Parties and Unrepresented Persons

text:
Lawyer Dmitri acts for the defendant in a personal injury action. The plaintiff is self-represented. During an early case-management conference, the plaintiff asks Dmitri to explain 'how the limitation period works.' Dmitri tells the plaintiff: 'Generally, there is a two-year limitation period in Ontario for personal injury claims. Whether your claim was filed in time is something you should confirm with a lawyer.' The plaintiff then asks: 'So is my claim okay? I filed it about two years and one month after the accident.' Dmitri is considering how to respond to that second question.

How should Dmitri respond to the plaintiff's second question under Rule 7.2-9, and what is the line between permissible information and prohibited advice?
opts: Dmitri may answer the second question honestly by telling the plaintiff whether the claim appears to be filed within the limitation period, since providing accurate legal information benefits the administration of justice and does not constitute legal advice.
Dmitri must refuse all engagement with the plaintiff about limitation periods because raising the topic at all gives the plaintiff an unfair advantage over the defendant.

Dmitri must not answer the second question; telling the plaintiff whether their specific claim is or is not time-barred is legal advice — applying the law to the plaintiff's facts — which Dmitri is prohibited from giving to an adverse unrepresented party; Dmitri should reiterate that the plaintiff should consult a lawyer about their specific situation.

Dmitri may answer the second question because the plaintiff asked directly and any refusal would be misleading; an unrepresented party who asks a direct legal question is entitled to an honest direct answer from opposing counsel.
expl: Rule 7.2-9 and its Commentary establish that a lawyer dealing with an unrepresented opposing party may provide basic legal information — general explanations of how legal processes work, where to find court offices, what types of timelines generally exist. However, the lawyer must not give the unrepresented person legal advice — that is, apply the law to the specific facts of their situation and tell them what their rights are or what they should do. Dmitri's first response (explaining that a two-year limitation period generally exists and recommending the plaintiff verify with a lawyer) is basic legal information. The second question — whether this specific claim, filed two years and one month after the accident, is timely — is a request for legal advice: it asks Dmitri to assess the plaintiff's specific circumstances against the limitation period. Answering it would require applying the Limitations Act, 2002 to the facts and offering an opinion on the plaintiff's legal position. That is prohibited. Dmitri must decline and again direct the plaintiff to seek counsel.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-9 Commentary (basic information vs. legal advice to unrepresented party)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 8 | Dealing with Opposing Parties and Unrepresented Persons

text:
Lawyer Nadia is acting for a defendant in a civil action. Discoveries are complete. A pre-trial conference is scheduled in one week and trial is set to begin in four weeks. The client has refused to pay any fees for the past three months and has ceased communicating with Nadia. Nadia wants to withdraw immediately by filing a Notice of Intention to Act in Person (since the client is going unrepresented). Opposing counsel objects that court leave is required.

Is court leave required for Nadia to withdraw from a civil matter four weeks before trial where the client will be unrepresented?
opts: No — court leave is only required in criminal proceedings; for civil matters a lawyer may always withdraw by filing the appropriate notice without court permission.

Yes — while court leave is not always required in civil matters, where withdrawal at a late stage would leave the opposing party's case at significant risk of prejudice and the trial is imminent, a court may require leave; given the four-week timeline, the pre-trial conference, and the risk that the unrepresented defendant will be unable to prepare adequately, Nadia should bring a motion for leave rather than simply filing a notice.
No — because the client has ceased communicating, Nadia is automatically entitled to withdraw immediately without any procedure.
No — Nadia can withdraw by filing the Notice of Intention to Act in Person; court leave is not required in civil proceedings under any circumstances.
expl: While court leave to withdraw is not universally required in civil proceedings, courts have inherent jurisdiction to require counsel to seek leave when withdrawal at a critical juncture would prejudice the administration of justice or leave a party without adequate opportunity to prepare. Four weeks before trial, with a pre-trial conference imminent, and the client becoming unrepresented, is precisely the kind of situation where Nadia should bring a motion rather than simply filing a notice. The court can then consider whether to grant leave, impose conditions, or order a short adjournment. Filing a notice without leave in these circumstances risks an order requiring Nadia to continue.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.7-8 (withdrawal from proceedings — civil matters near trial)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 | Withdrawal from Representation

text:
Lawyer Elena is defence counsel in a multi-week fraud trial that has been ongoing for three weeks. Elena has a personal conflict with her client that she believes makes it impossible to continue the representation. She would like to withdraw immediately. The trial is adjourned for a one-week break before evidence continues.

What procedural steps must Elena take before withdrawing from a criminal matter that is mid-trial?
opts: Elena may withdraw at any time during a trial recess without any formalities because the adjournment provides an opportunity for new counsel to step in.
Elena must file a notice of withdrawal with the LSO before withdrawing from the criminal matter.

In a criminal proceeding, a lawyer who wishes to withdraw mid-trial must generally bring a motion before the trial court and seek leave to withdraw; the court has the authority to refuse leave or impose conditions to protect the accused's right to a fair trial and to prevent prejudice to the proceedings; Elena cannot simply withdraw without court permission during a mid-trial adjournment.
Elena may withdraw during the adjournment without court permission because the adjournment creates a natural break in the proceedings that gives the court implied permission for counsel changes.
expl: Once a criminal trial has commenced, a lawyer who wishes to withdraw requires leave from the court. This is because the accused's right to a fair trial and the court's interest in maintaining the orderly conduct of proceedings are engaged once a trial is underway. The court may refuse leave or impose conditions — such as requiring Elena to complete the examination of a particular witness — before permitting withdrawal. A trial adjournment does not constitute implicit court permission to change counsel; formal leave is required.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.7-8 (withdrawal from criminal proceedings — court permission)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 | Withdrawal from Representation

text:
Lawyer Tanya has been acting for a plaintiff in a personal injury trial that is in its fifth day. The client owes $40,000 in unpaid fees. Tanya has given proper written notice to the client three weeks earlier that she would withdraw if fees were not paid, but no payment has been made. The client has been under cross-examination by opposing counsel for two days and is expected to be excused from the stand tomorrow. Tanya now wants to withdraw mid-trial.

May Tanya withdraw mid-trial at this point on the basis of non-payment, given that she gave proper advance notice?
opts: Yes — Tanya gave proper notice three weeks in advance; she has fulfilled all requirements for withdrawal and may now withdraw at any point, including mid-trial.
Yes — non-payment of fees is an absolute ground for withdrawal that overrides any timing concerns once proper notice has been given.

No — even though non-payment is a valid ground for withdrawal and proper notice was given, Rule 3.7-1 requires that a lawyer not withdraw at a time or in a manner that would cause prejudice to the client; withdrawing mid-trial while the client is under cross-examination is precisely the kind of prejudicial timing the rule prohibits unless mandatory grounds exist.
No — a lawyer can never withdraw from a civil trial once it has started, regardless of the grounds.
expl: Rule 3.7-1 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct recognizes non-payment as a ground for optional withdrawal but requires that the withdrawal be timed so as not to cause prejudice to the client. Withdrawing while the client is mid-cross-examination in a personal injury trial is extraordinarily prejudicial — it would leave the client without counsel at the most critical and vulnerable moment of the proceeding. The fact that Tanya gave prior notice does not override the timing constraint. Proper notice three weeks ago may have been sufficient for a transactional matter, but litigation requires that timing minimize prejudice. Mandatory withdrawal grounds (such as client fraud) could justify withdrawal even mid-trial, but non-payment is only a permissive ground and carries the additional constraint of appropriate timing.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.7-1 (withdrawal — timing constraint — no prejudice to client)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 | Withdrawal from Representation

text:
Lawyer Zara has been representing a client in a fraud matter. During the course of the retainer, Zara discovers that the client has been using Zara's legal services as part of a scheme to commit mortgage fraud — Zara has unknowingly been facilitating fraudulent transactions. Upon discovering this, the client admits the fraud to Zara and instructs her to continue. Zara considers her options.

Under Rule 3.7-7 of the Rules of Professional Conduct, is Zara required to withdraw from the retainer?
