opts: No — Zara may continue because she was not aware of the fraud at the outset of the retainer.
No — Zara may continue as long as she no longer assists with any new fraudulent transactions and limits her work to legitimate aspects of the retainer.

Yes — Rule 3.7-7 requires mandatory withdrawal when the client is using or has used the lawyer's services to facilitate crime or fraud; Zara must withdraw immediately and may not continue the retainer.
Yes — but Zara may continue until she has found replacement counsel for the client, as immediate withdrawal would prejudice the client.
expl: Rule 3.7-7 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct requires a lawyer to withdraw from a retainer if the lawyer discovers that the client has been using the lawyer's services to commit a crime or fraud. This is a mandatory withdrawal ground — it is not optional. Zara must withdraw immediately. The fact that she was unaware of the fraud at the outset does not permit her to continue once the fraud is discovered. Zara must also take care on withdrawal to comply with Rule 3.7-9 (reasonable notice, file transfer) while not facilitating further fraudulent conduct.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.7-7
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 | Withdrawal from Representation

text:
Lawyer Cordelia is defending a client charged with assault causing bodily harm. At a pre-trial conference, Cordelia advises the client that his proposed testimony directly contradicts all physical and digital evidence and that Cordelia believes the proposed evidence would be perjured. The client insists he will testify as planned. Cordelia advises the client again in writing that she cannot continue if he intends to give false evidence. The client is unmoved and instructs Cordelia to proceed.

What must Cordelia do under Rule 3.7-7 when a client persists in the intention to give perjured testimony?
opts: Cordelia may continue the representation because she has fulfilled her obligation by advising the client in writing of the professional consequences.
Cordelia may continue but must refrain from calling the client as a witness; she may present the defence through other means without eliciting the false testimony.

Cordelia must withdraw from the representation; Rule 3.7-7 requires mandatory withdrawal where the client persists in an instruction that would require the lawyer to engage in conduct that is dishonest or that would mislead the court; a lawyer cannot continue to represent a client who insists on giving testimony the lawyer believes to be perjured.
Cordelia may continue if she applies to the court for leave to cross-examine her own client to challenge the false testimony during the client's examination-in-chief.
expl: Rule 3.7-7 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct requires mandatory withdrawal in circumstances where the client persists in conduct that would require the lawyer to mislead the court or engage in dishonest conduct. A client who insists on giving testimony the lawyer believes to be perjured falls squarely within this category. Cordelia cannot continue — she must withdraw. The fact that she has warned the client does not authorize her to continue the retainer and facilitate or allow the false testimony to proceed. The only permissible course is withdrawal.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.7-7 (mandatory withdrawal — client persists in perjury)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 | Withdrawal from Representation

text:
Lawyer Dante has been representing a client in a family law matter for 10 months. The client owes $22,000 in unpaid fees. Dante has sent three invoices, each with payment due in 30 days, all of which have been ignored. Dante has not given the client any specific warning that he intends to withdraw if payment is not received. He now wants to withdraw immediately without any further notice.

What steps must Dante take before withdrawing on the basis of non-payment of fees under Rule 3.7-1?
opts: Dante may withdraw immediately upon discovering non-payment; no additional steps are required once fees are overdue.

Dante must give the client reasonable notice of his intention to withdraw for non-payment, including a clear statement that the retainer will be terminated if fees are not paid; he must give the client a reasonable opportunity to pay or make alternative arrangements before withdrawing.
Dante must first obtain a court order authorizing his withdrawal before he can terminate the retainer for non-payment.
Dante may withdraw immediately but must file a motion with the court to have the client declared liable for Dante's outstanding fees as a condition of withdrawal.
expl: Rule 3.7-1 recognizes non-payment of fees as a ground for optional withdrawal, but the right to withdraw is not automatic or immediate. The Commentary to Rule 3.7-1 requires that before withdrawing for non-payment, the lawyer must give the client reasonable notice of the intention to withdraw if fees are not paid and must provide the client a reasonable opportunity to address the non-payment. Withdrawal without any notice to the client — beyond having sent unpaid invoices — does not satisfy this requirement. Dante must specifically warn the client that the retainer will be terminated if fees are not paid, and then allow a reasonable time to respond.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.7-1 (non-payment withdrawal — reasonable notice required)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 | Withdrawal from Representation

text:
Lawyer Barbara has decided to withdraw from representing a client in an ongoing litigation matter. After giving the client notice of her withdrawal, the client requests the return of all original documents from the file, including documents Barbara received from third parties in the course of the retainer. Barbara wants to retain some documents as security for her unpaid fees.

What are Barbara's obligations regarding the client's file upon withdrawal under Rule 3.7-9?
opts: Barbara may retain all documents in the file as a solicitor's lien until the client pays all outstanding fees.
Barbara must return the entire file to the client immediately upon withdrawal and has no right to retain any documents.

Upon withdrawal, Barbara must return all property belonging to the client and provide sufficient notice to allow the client to retain new counsel; while Barbara may have a solicitor's lien over certain property for unpaid fees, she must not use the withholding of the file as a tool to pressure the client in a way that prejudices the client's legal interests.
Barbara may retain the entire file permanently because the documents were collected in the course of her retainer and therefore belong to her firm.
expl: Rule 3.7-9 requires a lawyer who withdraws to give reasonable notice to the client and to take steps to minimize prejudice to the client's interests. Upon withdrawal, the lawyer must return all property belonging to the client. A lawyer may have a solicitor's lien over some property for unpaid fees, but the exercise of that lien must not prejudice the client's legal position in the ongoing matter. The lawyer must carefully balance the lien right against the obligation not to cause prejudice.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.7-9
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 | Withdrawal from Representation

text:
Lawyer Felix decides to withdraw from acting for a client whose litigation matter is active in the courts. He sends the client a brief email that says only 'I am no longer your lawyer effective today.' He does not advise the client about upcoming deadlines, does not transfer the file to new counsel or to the client, and does not take any steps to ensure important court dates are not missed. There is a critical motion scheduled in three days.

Has Felix complied with his obligations on withdrawal under Rule 3.7-9?
opts: Yes — sending notice to the client is all that is required on withdrawal; the client is responsible for retaining new counsel to manage upcoming deadlines.

No — Rule 3.7-9 requires that on withdrawal a lawyer give reasonable notice to the client, take steps to avoid foreseeable prejudice to the client including advising of upcoming deadlines, and ensure the client has the information and time necessary to obtain new representation; Felix's one-line email with no information about upcoming events is inadequate.
Yes — sending a notice of withdrawal is sufficient as long as it is sent before the matter becomes urgent.
No — but Felix's only error was failing to file a notice of change of solicitor with the court before withdrawing; all other obligations are satisfied by the email.
expl: Rule 3.7-9 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct imposes a set of obligations on a lawyer who withdraws from a retainer. These include: giving the client reasonable notice of the withdrawal; taking steps to avoid foreseeable prejudice to the client, which includes advising about critical upcoming deadlines; and providing sufficient information to allow the client to retain new counsel in a timely manner. A bare one-line email that does not address an imminent motion three days away fails virtually all of these obligations. The client is left without information needed to protect their own legal interests.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.7-9 (withdrawal obligations — notice and avoiding prejudice)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 | Withdrawal from Representation

text:
Lawyer Archie has been acting for a client in a complex breach of contract action for 18 months. The trial is scheduled to begin in three weeks. The client has consistently ignored Archie's advice on litigation strategy and has given instructions that Archie believes are not in the client's best interests. The client has also failed to pay Archie's last three invoices totalling $15,000. Archie wants to withdraw from the retainer immediately.

Which of the following most accurately describes Archie's rights and obligations regarding withdrawal under Rule 3.7-1?
opts: Archie must continue representing the client until the trial concludes because withdrawal this close to trial would prejudice the client, regardless of non-payment or strategic disagreements.
Archie may withdraw immediately without any obligation to the client because non-payment of fees is an absolute right to withdraw.

Under Rule 3.7-1, non-payment of fees and loss of confidence in the lawyer-client relationship are grounds for optional withdrawal, but Archie must give reasonable notice and withdraw at an appropriate time in the proceedings to minimize prejudice to the client; a three-week window before trial requires Archie to consider whether immediate withdrawal is appropriate or whether adequate time remains to find replacement counsel.
Archie cannot withdraw at all because a lawyer must always see a matter to completion once retained.
expl: Rule 3.7-1 provides grounds for optional withdrawal, including loss of confidence in the lawyer-client relationship and client failure to pay fees. However, the right to withdraw is constrained by the obligation to give reasonable notice and to withdraw at a time and in a manner that minimizes prejudice to the client. With trial three weeks away, Archie must carefully assess whether withdrawal at this stage would prejudice the client's ability to obtain replacement counsel and prepare for trial. The closer to trial, the more compelling the reason for withdrawal must be.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.7-1
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 | Withdrawal from Representation

text:
Lawyer Miriam decides to withdraw from a civil litigation retainer. She gives proper notice of withdrawal. On her last day, she accounts for all trust funds and returns the balance to the client, delivers the full client file to the client with a letter identifying outstanding deadlines, and writes to the opposing lawyer advising that she has withdrawn and that the client is seeking new counsel. She asks the opposing lawyer for a 30-day extension on an upcoming motion to allow the client time to retain new counsel.

Which of the following best describes Miriam's conduct with respect to her obligations on withdrawal under Rule 3.7-9?
opts: Miriam has done more than is required; a lawyer who withdraws has no obligation to assist the client after the retainer ends.

Miriam has complied with her obligations under Rule 3.7-9; the rule requires a withdrawing lawyer to give reasonable notice, account for trust funds, return the file, advise of outstanding matters, and take steps to protect the client from prejudice — all of which Miriam has done.
Miriam's withdrawal is improper because she should have sought court approval before notifying the opposing lawyer.
Miriam has not complied because she was required to find replacement counsel for her client before withdrawing.
expl: Rule 3.7-9 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct sets out the obligations a lawyer must fulfill on withdrawal: give reasonable notice to the client; account for all trust funds; return all client property; advise the client of upcoming deadlines and steps necessary to protect their interests; and take steps to minimize prejudice to the client, which may include notifying opposing counsel and seeking extensions to allow the client time to retain new counsel. Miriam has fulfilled all of these obligations. She is not required to find a replacement lawyer — her obligation is to take reasonable steps to protect the client's interests during the transition.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.7-9 (obligations on withdrawal — protecting client interests)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 | Withdrawal from Representation

text:
Lawyer Omar has been acting for the defendant in a civil action. Discoveries are complete and a trial date has been set for six months from now. Omar and his client have had a serious falling out — the client has written Omar an abusive letter and announced she is planning to retain new counsel. Omar wishes to formally withdraw from the record.

What procedural step is required for Omar to withdraw from a civil litigation matter that is on the record with a trial date?
opts: Omar may file a Notice of Change of Solicitor immediately and his withdrawal is complete upon filing.
Omar must bring a motion to the court for leave to withdraw and cannot be removed from the record until the court grants leave.
Omar must obtain the client's signed consent and then file the appropriate notice; once the client has announced she is retaining new counsel, no court leave is required.
Omar must remain as counsel of record until the new lawyer files a Notice of Change of Solicitor on behalf of the client; he cannot withdraw unilaterally.
expl: In Ontario civil litigation, the procedure for withdrawal depends on the circumstances. Where a client is retaining new counsel and will not be left unrepresented, and where the client consents, the standard procedure is for the new lawyer to file a Notice of Change of Solicitor — or for the withdrawing lawyer to file a Notice of Intention to Act in Person if the client will be self-represented. Court leave is generally required only when the client is not obtaining new representation and would be left unrepresented at a critical stage, or in criminal proceedings. In Omar's case, since the client has announced she is retaining new counsel, the appropriate path is for the new counsel to file the change notice; no court motion is required.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.7-8 (withdrawal from proceedings — civil vs. criminal distinction)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 | Withdrawal from Representation

text:
Lawyer Devon has been acting for a client in a corporate commercial matter involving the negotiation of a shareholders agreement. No proceedings have been filed in any court. The client has breached the retainer by failing to follow Devon's reasonable legal advice on multiple occasions, and the working relationship has completely broken down. There are no immediate deadlines — the next meeting with the other side is in six weeks.

In these circumstances, is Devon entitled to withdraw from the transactional retainer under Rule 3.7-1?
opts: No — a lawyer cannot withdraw from a transactional matter unless the client consents to the withdrawal.

Yes — loss of confidence in the lawyer-client relationship and client failure to follow reasonable legal advice are grounds for optional withdrawal under Rule 3.7-1; with no immediate deadlines and six weeks until the next step, withdrawal at this time is unlikely to cause prejudice and Devon may withdraw on reasonable notice.
No — Devon must complete the shareholders agreement before withdrawing because the client has already paid fees for this work.
Yes — but only if Devon can find replacement counsel for the client before withdrawing.
expl: Rule 3.7-1 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct permits optional withdrawal in circumstances including loss of confidence in the lawyer-client relationship and client failure to follow reasonable legal advice. The key constraint is that withdrawal must not cause prejudice to the client. In a transactional matter with no litigation, no court proceedings, and no immediate deadlines for six weeks, withdrawal at this stage is not prejudicial — the client has ample time to retain new counsel and continue the transaction. Devon is entitled to withdraw on reasonable notice in these circumstances.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.7-1 (optional withdrawal — transactional matter — no prejudice)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 | Withdrawal from Representation

text:
Lawyer Franco gave a written undertaking to opposing counsel in a commercial transaction that he would deliver executed share transfer documents within five business days of closing. The closing occurred. Franco's client provided the documents but they were signed with an incorrect date. Correcting the error required the client to re-execute the documents, which the client refused to do for six weeks, citing travel commitments. The undertaking expired without performance. Opposing counsel complains to the LSO.

How is Franco's breach of undertaking likely to be treated by the LSO, and what remedies does opposing counsel have?
opts: The LSO is unlikely to take action because the breach was caused by the client's refusal to re-execute — client fault is a complete defence to a breach of undertaking complaint.

Breach of an undertaking is treated as professional misconduct regardless of the cause of non-performance; the fact that the client refused to cooperate does not excuse the breach because Franco's undertaking was his personal obligation; opposing counsel may also seek specific enforcement of the undertaking or damages in court.
The LSO will treat the breach as a civil dispute between the parties, not a professional responsibility matter, because the breach arose from a contractual performance issue.
Opposing counsel's only remedy is to sue Franco for breach of contract; the LSO does not deal with undertaking disputes.
expl: A lawyer's undertaking is a personal professional obligation under Rule 7.2-11. The reason for non-performance — including client fault, client refusal, or third-party non-cooperation — does not excuse the breach. Franco should not have given the undertaking if he could not personally guarantee performance. The LSO treats breach of undertaking as professional misconduct, often regardless of whether the non-performance was deliberate. In addition to professional discipline, courts in Ontario have the power to enforce a lawyer's undertaking directly by ordering the lawyer to perform, or opposing counsel may sue for damages caused by the breach.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 (breach of undertaking — professional misconduct; court enforcement)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 | Undertakings

text:
Lawyer Brett receives a package of confidential financial records from opposing counsel in a civil dispute, accompanied by a trust condition letter stating: 'These documents are produced to you on the condition that they will be used solely for the purposes of this litigation and will not be shared with any third parties or used for any collateral purpose.' Brett reviews the documents and realizes they contain highly useful information for a different matter he is handling for a related client. He decides to use the documents for that other matter while retaining them for the current litigation.

Is Brett's use of the documents for the collateral matter consistent with the trust condition imposed when the documents were delivered?
opts: Yes — once documents are received by a lawyer in litigation, they may be used for any legitimate legal purpose; trust conditions are advisory only and cannot restrict how a receiving lawyer uses documents.

No — a trust condition received by a lawyer is a binding professional obligation; Brett must use the documents only for the purpose specified in the condition; using them for any other matter is a breach of the trust condition and may constitute professional misconduct.
Yes — as long as Brett keeps the documents confidential and does not share them externally, he may use them for related matters; the trust condition only restricts disclosure to third parties.
No — but Brett's only obligation is to return the documents once they are no longer needed for the current litigation; he may use them for the collateral matter in the interim.
expl: A trust condition received by a lawyer creates a binding professional obligation on the receiving lawyer to use the materials only in accordance with the terms of the condition. The condition here is clear: sole use for this litigation, no use for collateral purposes. Brett's use of the documents for a different matter directly violates the trust condition. If Brett is unwilling to be bound by the conditions, his obligation is to return the documents immediately — he cannot accept the documents while rejecting the conditions attached to them. Using the documents in violation of the trust condition is a breach of a professional obligation and may expose Brett to professional misconduct proceedings.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 (trust conditions — receiving documents — must comply or return)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 | Undertakings

text:
In a residential real estate closing, the vendor's lawyer, Ingrid, is asked by the purchaser's lawyer to provide a solicitor's undertaking that the property will be free of all construction liens as of the closing date. Ingrid knows that a 45-day lien holdback period under the Construction Act has not yet expired and that there is a real possibility that unpaid subcontractors could file liens after closing. Ingrid's client tells her to give the undertaking to facilitate the closing.

Should Ingrid give the solicitor's undertaking as requested by the purchaser's lawyer?
opts: Yes — client authorization is sufficient grounds to give a solicitor's undertaking; Ingrid's obligation is to follow her client's instructions.
Yes — providing a solicitor's undertaking is a standard real estate closing practice and Ingrid is obligated to give it to facilitate the transaction.

No — Rule 7.2-11 requires that a lawyer only give an undertaking the lawyer is personally capable of fulfilling; since lien rights arise under the Construction Act and belong to third parties over whom Ingrid has no control, she cannot personally guarantee that the property will be lien-free; she should not give the undertaking in its current form.
Yes — Ingrid may give the undertaking and if any liens are filed after closing, she can simply discharge them from the holdback funds.
expl: Rule 7.2-11 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct requires a lawyer to give only those undertakings the lawyer is personally able to honor. Construction lien rights under the Construction Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.30 belong to subcontractors and suppliers who are third parties entirely outside Ingrid's control. If any of them register a lien after closing, Ingrid cannot personally prevent it. Giving an undertaking that she cannot personally guarantee exposes her to professional misconduct liability. The appropriate mechanism is to negotiate a holdback arrangement or other protective mechanism in the transaction, not to give an unperformable undertaking.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 (undertaking — personally capable of performance)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 | Undertakings

text:
In a civil dispute, Lawyer Faye acts for the plaintiff. In exchange for the plaintiff releasing original business records to the defendant's lawyer on a without-prejudice basis, opposing counsel gives Faye a written undertaking to return all original documents within 30 days regardless of the outcome of any negotiations. Thirty-five days later, the documents have not been returned and opposing counsel has stopped responding to Faye's requests. Faye asks her client how to enforce the undertaking.

What remedies are available to Faye when the opposing lawyer fails to honor a written undertaking to return documents?
opts: Faye's only remedy is to commence a civil action against the opposing lawyer for breach of contract; the LSO does not handle disputes about documents between lawyers.

Faye may file a complaint with the LSO reporting the breach of undertaking, which is professional misconduct; she may also bring a summary court application to enforce the undertaking directly without requiring full civil litigation; additionally, damages may be recoverable if the client has suffered loss from the breach.
Faye should write a strongly worded letter to the opposing lawyer; if that does not work, she must wait until the civil proceedings are complete before seeking enforcement of the undertaking.
Faye may only seek enforcement through the LSO complaints process; courts have no jurisdiction to enforce undertakings between lawyers because they are professional obligations, not legal contracts.
expl: When an opposing lawyer fails to honor a written undertaking, the aggrieved party has multiple avenues of redress. First, the breach of undertaking is professional misconduct, and a complaint may be filed with the LSO. Second, Ontario courts have recognized their inherent jurisdiction to summarily enforce lawyers' undertakings — Faye can bring a court application directly without going through full civil litigation. This is a well-established remedy that allows rapid enforcement. Third, if the client has suffered damages from the breach (such as loss caused by inability to use the original documents), civil damages may be recoverable. All three remedies may be pursued simultaneously.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 (breach of undertaking — LSO complaint, court enforcement, damages)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 | Undertakings

text:
In a commercial real estate transaction, Lawyer Asha is acting for the purchaser. The vendor's lawyer, opposing counsel in the transaction, gives a written undertaking that the existing first mortgage on the property will be discharged within 10 business days of closing. Relying on this undertaking, Asha advises her client to close without receiving a discharge statement. Twelve days after closing, the mortgage has not been discharged. Asha's client asks whether the undertaking can be enforced and whether a court order is required.

Is a lawyer's undertaking enforceable as a professional commitment without requiring a separate court order, and what mechanisms are available when an undertaking is not honored?
opts: No — a lawyer's undertaking is enforceable only if it is contained in a signed contract between the parties; without a contract, the undertaking has no legal force.

Yes — a lawyer's undertaking is enforceable as a personal professional commitment; the receiving lawyer may rely on the undertaking without requiring a court order; when the undertaking is not honored, the receiving lawyer may report the breach to the LSO and may seek court enforcement of the undertaking as a summary proceeding.
No — the receiving lawyer bears the risk of relying on an undertaking; if the other lawyer does not perform, the only remedy is a civil claim for damages.
Yes — but the undertaking can only be enforced by the LSO disciplinary process; courts have no jurisdiction to enforce a lawyer's undertaking directly.
expl: A lawyer's undertaking is a solemn personal commitment that is enforceable as a professional obligation without requiring a separate underlying contract. Courts in Ontario have recognized their inherent jurisdiction to summarily enforce lawyers' undertakings — a receiving lawyer can seek a court order compelling performance without going through the full civil litigation process. In addition, a breach of undertaking can be reported to the LSO as professional misconduct. The receiving lawyer is entitled to rely on the undertaking as a professional commitment and bears no obligation to demand additional security. Both avenues — LSO complaint and court enforcement — are available when an undertaking is not honored.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 Commentary (reliance on undertaking; court enforcement and LSO discipline)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 | Undertakings

text:
Lawyer Sandra acts for the vendor in a residential real estate closing. She gives a standard solicitor's undertaking to the purchaser's lawyer to discharge the existing mortgage on the property from the closing proceeds. Closing occurs and Sandra receives the full purchase price including sufficient funds to discharge the mortgage. However, she deposits the closing funds into the wrong trust account and does not process the mortgage discharge for three weeks, during which time the lender assesses an additional interest charge that reduces the available funds below the discharge amount. Sandra argues that the shortfall is the lender's fault for the interest calculation.

Is Sandra personally responsible for ensuring the mortgage discharge undertaking is honored, and does the lender's interest calculation excuse the shortfall?
opts: No — Sandra has fulfilled her undertaking by receiving sufficient funds at closing; the subsequent interest charge is the lender's responsibility and releases Sandra from the undertaking.

Yes — Sandra's solicitor's undertaking to discharge the mortgage is her personal professional obligation; she must honor it regardless of the interest shortfall caused by her delay; she must make up any shortfall from her own resources if necessary, and the delay was caused by her error in depositing the funds incorrectly.

No — the undertaking becomes unenforceable when the lender's actions (charging interest) make it impossible to perform with the available funds; Sandra may discharge her obligation by returning the remaining funds to the purchaser.
Yes — Sandra is responsible, but only for the original amount of the mortgage balance at closing; interest charges accrued after closing are outside the scope of the undertaking.
expl: Sandra's solicitor's undertaking to discharge the mortgage is her personal professional commitment. She received sufficient funds at closing to perform the undertaking. The subsequent interest shortfall arose directly from her own error in depositing funds into the wrong trust account and delaying the discharge process for three weeks. A lawyer who causes the conditions that make an undertaking impossible to perform from available funds cannot escape professional responsibility by attributing the consequences to the other party's actions. Sandra must honor the undertaking, including making up any shortfall resulting from her own delay, and she must process the discharge promptly.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 (real estate — mortgage discharge undertaking — personal obligation)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 | Undertakings

text:
Lawyer Fatima acts for the defendant in a civil proceeding. Opposing counsel, Lawyer Yusuf, sends Fatima a draft settlement agreement with a covering trust condition letter that reads: 'This draft is delivered to you in trust on the condition that it is to be shown to your client for approval purposes only and is not to be filed with any court, disclosed to any third party, or used for any purpose other than obtaining your client's instructions.' Fatima's client, after reviewing the draft, instructs Fatima to file the draft with the court as part of a motion record and also to provide a copy to a litigation funding company that is reviewing whether to fund the client's claim.

What must Fatima do in response to her client's instructions, given the trust condition under which the document was received?
opts: Fatima must follow her client's instructions; once a document is in her possession and her client has directed her to use it, client autonomy overrides any restriction imposed by opposing counsel.

Fatima must comply with the trust condition and must not file the draft with the court or provide it to the litigation funder; the trust condition is a binding professional obligation on her personally from the moment she accepted the document, and client instructions cannot override it.
Fatima may file the draft with the court if she first seeks and obtains leave from the court; judicial authorization supersedes a trust condition imposed by a private lawyer.

Fatima should ask Yusuf to modify the trust condition; if Yusuf refuses, Fatima may proceed on her client's instructions because a trust condition that is overly restrictive cannot be enforced against a receiving lawyer.
expl: A trust condition imposed by a delivering lawyer on documents or funds binds the receiving lawyer personally and immediately upon acceptance. Fatima accepted the draft settlement agreement subject to the trust condition that it was to be used only for obtaining client instructions and was not to be filed with any court or disclosed to third parties. That condition restricts the permissible use of the document absolutely within the stated scope. Fatima's client has now instructed her to use the document in ways that plainly exceed the trust condition — filing it with the court and sharing it with a litigation funder are both prohibited by the condition's terms. The critical point, and the Ethics Override Trap, is that client instruction cannot override the trust condition. The trust condition runs between Fatima personally and Yusuf as a professional obligation — it is not subject to modification by the client. If Fatima cannot comply with the condition (i.e., if her client's instructions make it impossible to proceed), her options are to seek Yusuf's agreement to vary the condition, or to withdraw from the file if client instructions are irreconcilable with the condition. She must not simply act on client direction in breach of the condition.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 (trust conditions — binding on receiving lawyer; client instruction cannot override)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 | Undertakings

text:
In a real estate transaction, Lawyer Claude gives a written undertaking to the purchaser's lender that the mortgage funds will be used to discharge all existing encumbrances on the property. After closing, Claude's client (the vendor) refuses to authorize the discharge of one of the encumbrances, leaving it on title. Claude argues to the lender that he cannot fulfill the undertaking because his client refused to cooperate.

Is Claude's argument that he cannot perform the undertaking because his client refused to cooperate a valid defence to his obligation?
opts: Yes — a lawyer's undertaking is only as binding as the client's instructions; if the client refuses to cooperate, the lawyer is released from the undertaking.
No — a lawyer's undertaking is the personal obligation of the lawyer giving it, not the client's obligation; Claude is bound to fulfill the undertaking regardless of whether his client cooperates.
Yes — the undertaking is void because it was conditional on the client's cooperation, which is an implied term.
No — but Claude is only obligated to make best efforts to perform; if the client refuses, best efforts satisfy the undertaking.
expl: A lawyer's undertaking is a solemn personal commitment of the lawyer — it is not the client's promise. When a lawyer gives an undertaking, they are personally bound to fulfill it regardless of whether the client cooperates. The LSO's Commentary on undertakings and Rule 7.2-11 make clear that a lawyer must only give an undertaking they are personally able to honor. If fulfillment depends on client cooperation that cannot be guaranteed, the lawyer should not give the undertaking in the first place. Claude's failure to fulfill the undertaking is a breach of a personal professional obligation.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 | Undertakings

text:
In a commercial transaction, opposing counsel asks Lawyer Dmitri to provide a solicitor's undertaking that his client will deliver a specific document within five business days of closing. Dmitri knows that producing the document depends on his client obtaining information from a third party over whom the client has no control. Dmitri's client tells him to give the undertaking anyway.

What should Dmitri do in accordance with Rule 7.2-11?
opts: Dmitri should give the undertaking because his client has authorized it; client authorization is the only requirement.
Dmitri should give the undertaking and include a note that performance depends on a third party, which converts it from an absolute undertaking to a conditional undertaking.

Dmitri must not give the undertaking because he cannot personally guarantee performance; Rule 7.2-11 requires that a lawyer only give an undertaking they are personally able to honour; the appropriate mechanism is a client undertaking or covenant in the agreement, not a solicitor's undertaking.
Dmitri may give the undertaking and then seek to negotiate an extension once closing occurs if the document is not delivered in time.
expl: Rule 7.2-11 requires a lawyer to give only those undertakings the lawyer is personally able to honor. Since delivery of the document depends on a third party over whom Dmitri's client has no control, Dmitri cannot personally guarantee performance. Giving the undertaking would expose Dmitri to professional misconduct liability if performance fails. The correct approach is to decline to give a solicitor's undertaking and instead have the client give a contractual covenant in the transaction documents, or make delivery conditional on the third party's cooperation with appropriate language.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 | Undertakings

text:
Lawyer Elise gave a written undertaking to opposing counsel that she would deliver signed transfer documents by a specified date. Elise forgot about the undertaking. The deadline passed without delivery, causing the opposing party to incur additional legal costs and a delay fee from the lender. Elise argues that the breach was inadvertent and therefore does not constitute professional misconduct.

Is Elise's argument that inadvertent breach of an undertaking is not professional misconduct correct?
opts: Yes — professional misconduct for breach of undertaking requires deliberate refusal to perform; inadvertent failure is merely a civil matter.
Yes — because the undertaking was not fulfilled due to oversight rather than bad faith, no professional responsibility consequence arises.

No — a lawyer who breaches an undertaking is generally subject to professional misconduct regardless of whether the breach was deliberate or inadvertent; a lawyer is required to fulfil their undertakings and must have adequate systems to ensure compliance; inadvertence is a mitigating factor, not a complete defence.
No — but liability for breach of an undertaking only extends to the additional legal costs incurred by opposing counsel, not to other consequential losses.
expl: Under the Rules of Professional Conduct and well-established LSO disciplinary jurisprudence, failure to honor an undertaking constitutes professional misconduct regardless of whether the failure was deliberate or inadvertent. A lawyer is required to put in place adequate systems to track and honor undertakings. Inadvertence may be a mitigating factor in assessing the appropriate penalty, but it is not a complete defence to professional misconduct. Elise's argument is incorrect — she is professionally responsible for the breach and must take steps to remedy the consequences.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 | Undertakings

text:
Lawyer Chen is acting for a borrower in a mortgage refinancing. The lender is not represented by a lawyer and is a private individual. To facilitate the transaction, Chen gives a written undertaking directly to the private lender that he will register the new mortgage on title within five business days of receiving the mortgage funds. Chen registers the mortgage on day eight due to a processing delay at the land registry office. The private lender complains that the undertaking was not honored.

Does a lawyer's undertaking given directly to a non-lawyer (such as a private lender) carry the same binding professional force as one given to another lawyer?
opts: No — the binding professional force of an undertaking depends on whether the recipient is a lawyer; undertakings given to non-lawyers are merely contractual promises with no special professional significance.

Yes — a lawyer's undertaking carries the same binding professional force regardless of whether the recipient is a lawyer or a non-lawyer; Chen is professionally bound to honor the undertaking given to the private lender and a breach carries the same consequences as a breach of an undertaking to a fellow lawyer.
No — undertakings to non-lawyers are enforceable only as contracts, not as professional obligations; the LSO has no jurisdiction over Chen's conduct toward the private lender.
Yes — but the only consequence of breach is that the private lender may sue Chen for breach of contract; no LSO proceedings arise from undertakings given to non-lawyers.
expl: Rule 7.2-11 and the Commentary to the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct make clear that a lawyer's undertaking is a personal professional commitment regardless of whether it is given to another lawyer or to a non-lawyer such as a lender, registrar, or third party. The binding nature of the commitment and the professional misconduct consequences of breach are the same. A lawyer must only give undertakings to third parties that the lawyer is personally able to honor, and breach carries the same professional responsibility consequences. Chen's undertaking to the private lender is a professional commitment, and the breach — though caused by a registry delay — is a breach of a personal professional obligation.
caseRef: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 (undertakings to non-lawyers — same professional force)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 10 | Undertakings

text:
Lawyer Paul has received a letter from the Law Society of Ontario advising that a complaint has been filed against him and that the LSO is investigating his conduct for alleged professional misconduct arising from his handling of client trust funds. Paul's colleague advises him that the LSO can discipline him without any formal hearing and that he has no right to present a defence. Paul is unsure of his rights.

Which of the following most accurately describes the process by which the LSO would proceed if the investigation results in a referral to a formal hearing?
opts: The LSO may impose discipline summarily without a formal hearing if the evidence of misconduct is clear; no right to a hearing exists for findings of professional misconduct.

If the investigation results in a referral to a hearing, the matter proceeds before the Law Society Tribunal Hearing Division; the respondent lawyer has the right to notice of the allegations, the right to be represented by counsel, the right to make full answer and defence, and the right to appeal an adverse decision to the Divisional Court.
The LSO disciplinary process is entirely administrative; there is no right of appeal from a Tribunal decision.
The LSO may impose a reprimand summarily, but for suspension or revocation a formal hearing is required; below-reprimand consequences may be imposed without notice or hearing.
expl: Under the Law Society Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. L.8, a lawyer who is the subject of a formal discipline proceeding has procedural rights that are analogous to those in a quasi-judicial proceeding: notice of the allegations (the formal notice of application), the right to retain and instruct counsel, the right to make full answer and defence including calling evidence and cross-examining witnesses, and the right to appeal to the Divisional Court. The Law Society Tribunal Hearing Division is the adjudicative body. Decisions of the Hearing Division may be appealed to the Divisional Court. The LSO cannot impose significant sanctions without a formal process — procedural fairness is a core requirement.
caseRef: Law Society Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. L.8, ss. 34-49 (discipline process — hearing panel — rights of respondent — appeal to Divisional Court)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 | Complaints and Discipline

text:
A client believes her lawyer handled her estate matter incompetently and also charged excessive fees. She wants to take formal action. A colleague tells her she must sue the lawyer in court and that the Law Society only deals with criminal behaviour by lawyers. Another colleague tells her the Law Society will reimburse her directly if the complaint is successful.

Which of the following most accurately describes the LSO complaints process and what it can achieve for the client?
opts: The LSO complaints process is only available to clients who have suffered financial loss; complaints about service quality or excessive fees are not investigated.

The LSO complaints process allows any person to file a complaint about a lawyer's conduct; the LSO investigates complaints of professional misconduct, conduct unbecoming a licensee, or incompetence; a successful complaint may result in regulatory consequences for the lawyer, but the LSO complaints process does not award damages or directly reimburse the complainant for financial losses.
Only the Law Society itself may initiate complaints against lawyers; members of the public cannot file complaints directly.
The LSO complaints process automatically awards compensation to clients whose lawyers are found to have engaged in professional misconduct.
expl: The Law Society Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. L.8 establishes the LSO complaints and discipline process. Any person — including clients, opposing parties, judges, and members of the public — may file a complaint with the LSO about a lawyer's conduct. The LSO investigates complaints of professional misconduct, conduct unbecoming a licensee, or incompetence. A successful complaint may result in regulatory outcomes such as reprimand, conditions on practice, suspension, or revocation. However, the LSO complaints process is a regulatory proceeding — it does not award damages to complainants. If the client wants monetary compensation, she must pursue a civil claim or apply to the Lawyers' Fund for Client Compensation in eligible cases.
caseRef: Law Society Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. L.8, ss. 34-49 (complaints and discipline process)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 | Complaints and Discipline

text:
Lawyer Dale is a practicing family lawyer in Toronto. In the course of his family law practice, Dale knowingly assists a client in hiding assets from opposing counsel during financial disclosure. Separately, outside his practice, Dale is convicted of shoplifting merchandise worth $75 from a grocery store — an offence unrelated to his legal work. The LSO is investigating both incidents.

Which of the following correctly distinguishes the two categories of discipline that may apply to Dale's conduct under the Law Society Act?
opts: Both the asset-hiding and shoplifting incidents constitute professional misconduct because professional misconduct applies to any wrongful act by a lawyer regardless of whether it occurs in practice.

Knowingly assisting a client in hiding assets is professional misconduct because it occurs in the course of practice and violates professional obligations; the shoplifting conviction is conduct occurring outside practice but may constitute conduct unbecoming a licensee if it is sufficiently serious to reflect adversely on Dale's fitness or on the reputation of the profession.
The shoplifting conviction cannot be the subject of any LSO discipline because it is a matter for the criminal courts; the LSO only has jurisdiction over conduct that occurs in the practice of law.
Both incidents are conduct unbecoming a licensee because conduct unbecoming is a broader category that encompasses all forms of lawyer misconduct.
expl: The Law Society Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. L.8, s. 33 defines two relevant disciplinary categories. 'Professional misconduct' refers to conduct that occurs in the course of the practice of law and violates the obligations of a lawyer — such as knowingly assisting a client in concealing assets during financial disclosure. 'Conduct unbecoming a licensee' covers conduct that, while not necessarily occurring in practice, is so contrary to the expected standards of good character and integrity that it reflects adversely on the lawyer's fitness or on the profession. A criminal conviction for theft, even though it occurs outside practice, can fall into this category. The two categories are distinct and both may result in discipline.
caseRef: Law Society Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. L.8, s. 33 (professional misconduct vs. conduct unbecoming a licensee)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 | Complaints and Discipline

text:
Lawyer Rita has been experiencing severe depression and a substance use disorder for two years. Her clients have experienced significant delays and communication failures. Several clients have filed complaints. The LSO investigation finds that Rita's conduct issues are largely attributable to her mental health and addiction, rather than dishonest intent. A colleague suggests that Rita will face the same discipline process as any other misconduct case. Rita's counsel argues that there is a separate process that applies.

Is Rita's counsel correct that there is a distinct process for situations involving incapacity, and how does it differ from the standard professional misconduct process?
opts: No — all conduct issues are governed by the professional misconduct process regardless of the cause; mental health and addiction are relevant only as mitigating factors in the penalty phase.

Yes — the Law Society Act, s. 40 provides a distinct incapacity process for situations where a lawyer's ability to practise is impaired by physical or mental illness or addiction; unlike the misconduct process (which focuses on culpability and sanction), the incapacity process focuses on the lawyer's capacity to practise safely and may result in conditions on the licence, a practice monitor, or suspension until fitness is restored — with an emphasis on protection of the public and supporting the lawyer's recovery.
Yes — if a lawyer has a mental health diagnosis, the LSO has no jurisdiction to impose any sanction other than a temporary leave of absence.
No — the incapacity process applies only to physical disabilities; mental health and addiction issues are addressed through the professional misconduct process.
expl: The Law Society Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. L.8, s. 40 establishes a separate fitness to practise / incapacity process that is distinct from the professional misconduct process. The incapacity process applies where a lawyer's ability to practise law is impaired by physical or mental illness, addiction, or other conditions affecting capacity. The focus is on the lawyer's fitness to practise safely — not on culpability for wrongdoing. Outcomes may include conditions on the licence (such as supervised practice, mandatory treatment, reporting requirements), suspension until fitness is demonstrated, or other protective measures. This is fundamentally different from the misconduct process, which focuses on culpable conduct and sanctions. The two processes may run concurrently if there are elements of both misconduct and incapacity.
caseRef: Law Society Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. L.8, s. 40 (fitness to practise — incapacity — distinct from professional misconduct process)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 | Complaints and Discipline

text:
A lawyer, while attending a bar association event, becomes intoxicated and makes highly offensive and sexually harassing remarks to a junior lawyer. No client interests are involved and the conduct occurs entirely at a social event outside the office. The lawyer argues that because the conduct is private social conduct with no client involvement, it cannot constitute professional misconduct and is not subject to LSO regulation.

Is the lawyer correct that conduct at a private social event with no client involvement is outside the scope of LSO discipline?
opts: Yes — the LSO only regulates conduct that occurs in the course of legal practice and directly affects clients; social conduct at personal events cannot give rise to professional misconduct.
Yes — harassment at a social event is a matter for the human rights tribunal or criminal law, not the LSO.

No — the Law Society Act creates a category of 'conduct unbecoming a licensee' that may cover behaviour outside the strict practice of law that is sufficiently serious to reflect adversely on the lawyer's fitness to practise or on the profession; sexually harassing conduct toward a colleague at a professional event may constitute conduct unbecoming regardless of the absence of client involvement.
No — but only if the other lawyer files a human rights complaint that is first adjudicated; LSO discipline is a secondary process.
expl: The Law Society Act establishes two relevant categories for discipline: professional misconduct (conduct in the course of practice that violates professional obligations) and 'conduct unbecoming a licensee' (conduct, whether in practice or not, that is incompatible with the good character and conduct expected of a licensee). Conduct unbecoming may capture serious behaviour outside the practice of law — including harassment at professional events — that reflects adversely on the lawyer's fitness to hold a licence. The absence of a client victim does not shield the conduct from LSO scrutiny.
caseRef: Law Society Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. L.8, s. 33 (professional misconduct vs. conduct unbecoming a licensee)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 | Complaints and Discipline

text:
Consider three different scenarios involving lawyers:
(1) Lawyer A provides a thorough legal opinion but makes a genuine error in statutory interpretation that a competent lawyer would not have made, causing the client financial loss.
(2) Lawyer B knowingly assists her client in creating backdated documents to use in ongoing litigation.
(3) Lawyer C bills the client for 40 hours of work that actually took 15 hours, knowing the extra 25 hours are fabricated.
The LSO is assessing whether each scenario constitutes professional misconduct.

Which of the following most accurately describes how the LSO would classify these three scenarios?
opts: All three constitute professional misconduct because any conduct that harms a client is professional misconduct.
Scenario (1) is professional misconduct; Scenarios (2) and (3) are conduct unbecoming because they involve dishonesty outside the practice of law.

Scenario (1) may constitute negligence or incompetence but is not automatically professional misconduct; Scenarios (2) and (3) are professional misconduct because they involve knowing participation in fraud and dishonest billing — intentional violations of core professional obligations.
Only Scenario (3) is professional misconduct; Scenario (2) involves client conduct that the lawyer facilitated, which is the client's responsibility, and Scenario (1) is merely negligence.
expl: The Law Society Act and the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct draw a meaningful distinction between negligence and professional misconduct. A lawyer who makes a genuine interpretive error, even a significant one, may be negligent — but negligence alone does not automatically constitute professional misconduct unless it is of a sufficiently serious character. By contrast, knowingly assisting a client in creating backdated documents (fraud) and deliberately billing for time never worked (dishonest billing / theft from client) are intentional violations of core professional obligations that fall squarely within professional misconduct. Intentional dishonesty and fraud in the course of practice are hallmarks of professional misconduct.
caseRef: Law Society Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. L.8, s. 33; Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.1 (professional misconduct — examples: fraud assistance, dishonest billing)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 | Complaints and Discipline

text:
The Law Society Tribunal Hearing Division finds that Lawyer Novak engaged in professional misconduct by misappropriating $30,000 from his trust account to pay his own expenses, repaying the full amount before the hearing, and cooperating fully with the LSO investigation. Novak argues that because he repaid the funds and cooperated, the only available penalty is a reprimand.

Which of the following is the most accurate description of the penalties available to the Law Society Tribunal upon a finding of professional misconduct for misappropriation, and is Novak's argument correct?
opts: Novak is correct — repayment of misappropriated funds and cooperation are complete mitigating factors that limit the available penalty to a reprimand.

The Law Society Tribunal may impose any of its available penalties — including reprimand, suspension with conditions, and revocation — on a finding of professional misconduct; misappropriation of client funds is among the most serious forms of professional misconduct and typically results in severe penalties; repayment and cooperation are mitigating factors that may affect the severity of the penalty, but they do not limit the Tribunal to a reprimand.
The only available penalties on a finding of professional misconduct are reprimand and a fine; suspension and revocation require a separate criminal conviction.
The Tribunal must impose the least severe available penalty upon a finding of professional misconduct where the lawyer has repaid the misappropriated funds.
expl: The Law Society Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. L.8 grants the Law Society Tribunal a range of penalties upon a finding of professional misconduct, including reprimand, suspension (with or without conditions), and revocation of the licence. Misappropriation of client trust funds is treated as among the most serious professional misconduct, and LSO jurisprudence generally results in revocation or lengthy suspension even where funds are repaid. Repayment and cooperation are mitigating factors that may influence the Tribunal's assessment of penalty, but they do not limit the Tribunal to a reprimand, nor do they preclude revocation.
caseRef: Law Society Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. L.8, ss. 34-49 (penalties — reprimand, suspension, revocation)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 | Complaints and Discipline

text:
The Law Society Tribunal has found Lawyer Oona guilty of professional misconduct for repeatedly failing to communicate with clients, missing multiple court deadlines, and neglecting three separate files over a two-year period. Oona has no prior disciplinary record, has expressed genuine remorse, has taken a practice management course, and has made arrangements to compensate the affected clients. The LSO prosecutor is seeking a 12-month suspension; Oona's counsel seeks a reprimand only.

Which factors would the Law Society Tribunal weigh in determining the appropriate penalty under LSOA s.35?
opts: The Tribunal considers only the severity of the misconduct; personal circumstances of the lawyer are not relevant to penalty.

The Tribunal considers factors including the severity and nature of the misconduct, the number of instances, harm to clients, the lawyer's prior disciplinary record, demonstrated remorse and remedial steps taken, client compensation, and the need for specific and general deterrence; Oona's lack of prior record, remorse, and remedial steps are relevant mitigating factors even for serious neglect.
The Tribunal must impose the penalty requested by the LSO prosecutor unless the respondent lawyer can establish exceptional circumstances.
The Tribunal considers only whether the misconduct was intentional; since neglect is inadvertent, the maximum penalty available is a reprimand.
expl: Under LSOA s.35, the Law Society Tribunal has broad discretion in determining penalty and weighs a range of aggravating and mitigating factors. Aggravating factors include the seriousness of the misconduct, the number of incidents, harm to clients, and prior disciplinary record. Mitigating factors include genuine remorse, remedial steps taken (such as the practice management course), client compensation, and cooperation with the investigation. Neither the prosecutor's requested penalty nor the respondent's requested penalty binds the Tribunal. The Tribunal aims for a penalty proportionate to the misconduct that protects the public and maintains confidence in the profession.
caseRef: Law Society Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. L.8, s. 35 (penalty determination — factors)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 11 | Complaints and Discipline

text:
Lawyer Karen is acting for a client in a complex real estate transaction. During the transaction, Karen notices several unusual circumstances: the client insists on using cash for a large portion of the purchase price, cannot provide a coherent explanation for the source of funds, and has twice changed the beneficial owner information provided in initial client identification documents. Karen is concerned the transaction may involve proceeds of crime. She considers reporting the matter to FINTRAC under the Proceeds of Crime (Money Laundering) and Terrorist Financing Act.

Which of the following best describes the framework governing Karen's reporting obligations in this situation?
opts: Karen must immediately report the suspicious transaction to FINTRAC regardless of solicitor-client privilege; FINTRAC reporting obligations override privilege in all circumstances.
Karen has no reporting obligation because all communications with clients about financial matters are protected by solicitor-client privilege.

The Proceeds of Crime (Money Laundering) and Terrorist Financing Act imposes reporting obligations on certain professionals, but lawyers benefit from an exemption for information protected by solicitor-client privilege; Karen's reporting analysis must distinguish between information received in the course of seeking or giving legal advice (which is privileged) and information relating purely to the financial transaction itself; she must also consider whether to withdraw if she cannot complete the transaction ethically.
Karen is required to report only if the client explicitly admits the funds are proceeds of crime.
expl: The relationship between FINTRAC reporting obligations and solicitor-client privilege is complex. The Supreme Court of Canada has confirmed that solicitor-client privilege is a fundamental right that cannot be overridden by FINTRAC reporting requirements where information was received in the course of seeking or giving legal advice. However, lawyers do have obligations relating to their own participation in transactions — they cannot facilitate transactions they believe or suspect involve proceeds of crime. When Karen suspects the transaction involves money laundering, her obligations include: (1) carefully assessing whether the relevant information is protected by privilege; (2) refusing to complete the transaction if she cannot do so ethically; and (3) potentially withdrawing from representation. The obligation to report is not as straightforward as for non-legal professionals, given the privilege overlay.
caseRef: Proceeds of Crime (Money Laundering) and Terrorist Financing Act; LSO By-Law 7.1; Federation of Law Societies of Canada — solicitor-client privilege and FINTRAC
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12 | Client Identification and Verification

text:
Lawyer Amara is retained by a new client to assist with the purchase of a residential property. The transaction involves a financial transaction — specifically, the transfer of funds through Amara's trust account. Amara is very busy and postpones collecting the client's identification information, intending to get it before closing. The client does not provide identification at the initial meeting, and Amara proceeds to open the file and begin the work without obtaining it.

Under By-Law 7.1, s.20, when was Amara required to identify the client?
opts: Amara was required to identify the client before the closing, because that is when the financial transaction actually takes place.
Amara was required to identify the client before or as soon as practicable after being retained; delaying identification until just before closing breaches By-Law 7.1, s.20.
Amara was required to identify the client only if the transaction value exceeded $10,000.
Amara was required to identify the client only after the client's funds passed through the trust account.
expl: By-Law 7.1, s.20 requires a lawyer to identify every client before or as soon as practicable after being retained in a matter that involves a financial transaction. The obligation arises at the outset of the retainer, not at the point of closing. Amara's delay in obtaining identification information is a breach of By-Law 7.1, s.20. The identification obligation applies to all financial transactions regardless of dollar value — there is no $10,000 threshold for the identification requirement (though that threshold applies differently to the FINTRAC cash reporting regime).
caseRef: LSO By-Law 7.1, s.20 (client identification — when required)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12 | Client Identification and Verification

text:
Lawyer Benson is retained by a client, Cora, to assist with a commercial lease transaction. Benson asks Cora her full name, date of birth, and address at the initial meeting, and records this in his file. He does not ask to see any government-issued identification or take any steps to confirm that the information Cora provided is accurate. Benson believes he has satisfied his obligations under By-Law 7.1 because he has recorded Cora's personal information.

Has Benson satisfied all of his obligations under By-Law 7.1?
opts: Yes — collecting and recording the client's name, date of birth, and address satisfies both the identification and verification requirements under By-Law 7.1.

No — Benson has satisfied the identification obligation but not the verification obligation; By-Law 7.1 requires a lawyer to also verify the identity of individual clients, typically by reviewing an original government-issued photo ID.
Yes — verification of identity is only required for corporate clients, not individuals.
No — Benson must also verify the client's identity using a statutory declaration sworn before a commissioner of oaths.
expl: By-Law 7.1 draws a clear distinction between client identification (collecting information about the client) and client verification (taking steps to confirm that the information is accurate). Identification involves recording the client's name, date of birth, and address. Verification — required for individual clients in financial transaction matters — requires the lawyer to review an original government-issued photo ID, such as a passport or driver's licence. Benson recorded information but took no steps to confirm its accuracy, so while he has satisfied the identification step, he has not completed the verification step. The verification obligation is not limited to corporate clients.
caseRef: LSO By-Law 7.1, s.20-22 (identification vs. verification distinction)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12 | Client Identification and Verification

text:
Lawyer Diana is retained by an individual client, Emeka, who lives in Vancouver. The transaction requires client verification. Emeka is unable to travel to Toronto to meet Diana in person. Diana asks Emeka to send her a photocopy of his driver's licence by email. Diana reviews the photocopy and records it in her file, believing this satisfies her verification obligation.

Has Diana satisfied the client verification requirement under By-Law 7.1?
opts: Yes — reviewing a photocopy of a government-issued photo ID is sufficient for remote clients because the original cannot be presented in person.

No — where an individual client cannot appear in person, By-Law 7.1 requires a lawyer to use a prescribed alternative method such as the dual-process method; reliance on a photocopy alone does not satisfy the verification requirement.
Yes — reviewing a copy is sufficient provided the lawyer retains the copy in the client file.
No — Diana must require the client to attend her office in person regardless of the client's geographic location.
expl: By-Law 7.1, s.22 requires verification of individual client identity using an original government-issued photo ID. Where the client cannot appear in person — for example, because they are located in another province — the lawyer cannot simply accept a photocopy sent by email. Instead, By-Law 7.1 permits use of alternative prescribed methods, such as the dual-process method (using two independent sources, such as a credit report and a utility bill, to corroborate the client's identity). A photocopy alone, without the ability to inspect the original document, is insufficient for verification purposes.
caseRef: LSO By-Law 7.1, s.22 (verification — remote clients; dual-process method)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12 | Client Identification and Verification

text:
Lawyer Fiona has represented Greenhouse Corp., a public company listed on the Toronto Stock Exchange, in commercial disputes for the past six years. She has verified the corporation's identity on multiple prior matters. Greenhouse Corp. now retains Fiona on a new commercial litigation matter that will not involve any financial transaction through Fiona's trust account — Fiona will advise on litigation strategy and draft pleadings only. Fiona's assistant prepares a verification request for Greenhouse Corp., asking the client to re-provide its certificate of incorporation and beneficial ownership information.

Is Fiona required to re-verify Greenhouse Corp.'s identity for this new retainer?
opts: Yes — verification must be performed fresh on every new matter, even for long-standing clients, regardless of whether a financial transaction is involved.
Yes — public companies are subject to enhanced verification requirements and must always be re-verified.

No — By-Law 7.1 exempts lawyers from the verification obligation when acting for a publicly-listed company; and the verification obligation is also not triggered when the matter is limited to litigation with no financial transaction through trust.
No — but only because Fiona has already verified Greenhouse Corp. on prior matters; she must still document why re-verification is not required.
expl: By-Law 7.1 contains several exceptions to the verification obligation. Two apply here: (1) a lawyer is not required to verify client identity when acting for a public company listed on a recognized stock exchange, and (2) the verification obligation is not triggered in matters that are purely litigation-based with no financial transaction flowing through the lawyer's trust account. Both exceptions apply to Fiona's situation. This is an exam trap because candidates may assume verification is always required for corporate clients or that all new matters require fresh verification.
caseRef: LSO By-Law 7.1 (exceptions to verification — public companies; litigation-only matters)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12 | Client Identification and Verification

text:
Lawyer Ivan is retained by a new client, Jorge, who discloses that he is a former foreign minister of a South American country who has recently relocated to Canada. Jorge instructs Ivan on a substantial real estate investment involving multiple properties in Ontario. Ivan conducts standard client identification and verification steps — he records Jorge's name, date of birth, and address and reviews Jorge's passport. Ivan does not conduct any enhanced due diligence and does not inquire about the source of Jorge's funds.

Which of the following best describes the additional obligations Ivan has when acting for Jorge?
opts: Ivan's standard identification and verification steps are sufficient; By-Law 7.1 does not require any additional steps when acting for former government officials.

Ivan must conduct enhanced due diligence when acting for a politically exposed person (PEP), which includes taking reasonable steps to establish the source of funds, obtaining senior management approval, and implementing enhanced ongoing monitoring of the business relationship.
Ivan is not required to conduct enhanced due diligence because Jorge is a former official, not a current one; PEP obligations only apply to individuals currently holding public office.
Ivan must report Jorge to FINTRAC immediately because acting for a foreign government official is inherently suspicious.
expl: By-Law 7.1 and the anti-money laundering framework require enhanced due diligence when a lawyer determines that a client is a politically exposed person (PEP) or a head of an international organization. PEPs include current and former foreign government officials, including former ministers. The enhanced measures include taking reasonable steps to establish the source of the client's funds, obtaining senior management approval for the retainer, and implementing heightened ongoing monitoring of the business relationship. Enhanced due diligence reflects the elevated risk of money laundering or corruption associated with individuals who have held significant public positions and may have access to state resources.
caseRef: LSO By-Law 7.1 (PEPs — enhanced due diligence); Proceeds of Crime (Money Laundering) and Terrorist Financing Act
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12 | Client Identification and Verification

text:
Lawyer Gwen is retained by Redwood Holdings Inc. to assist with a commercial real estate acquisition. The sole representative who contacts Gwen is Marcus, who says he is the CEO of Redwood Holdings Inc. Gwen speaks with Marcus, records his name, and confirms his title as CEO via a company email. Gwen does not obtain any certificate of incorporation, corporate registry search, or any information about who beneficially owns or controls Redwood Holdings Inc.

Has Gwen satisfied the client identification and verification requirements for corporate clients under By-Law 7.1, s.23?
opts: Yes — confirming the identity and title of the corporate representative is sufficient verification for a corporate client.

No — By-Law 7.1, s.23 requires a lawyer to confirm the legal existence of the corporation (typically through a corporate registry search or certificate of incorporation) and to identify the corporation's directors and beneficial owners; Gwen has done neither.
Yes — verification of corporate identity is only required for private companies, not for companies that have an identifiable corporate representative.
No — but Gwen's obligation was satisfied when she recorded the CEO's name and corporate email, because a corporate email constitutes a form of official documentation.
expl: By-Law 7.1, s.23 establishes a multi-step process for verifying corporate client identity. A lawyer must: (1) confirm the legal existence of the corporation, typically through a current corporate registry search or certificate of incorporation; (2) obtain the names of the corporation's directors; and (3) identify the beneficial owners — i.e., individuals who own or control 25% or more of the corporation. Gwen has done none of this. Confirming a representative's title via corporate email does not establish that the corporation legally exists, who controls it, or whether there are any beneficial ownership concerns.
caseRef: LSO By-Law 7.1, s.23 (verification — corporations — existence and beneficial owners)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12 | Client Identification and Verification

text:
Lawyer Helen is retained by Nina, an individual who says she is acting as an agent and nominee for a principal named Oscar, who is the beneficial purchaser of a commercial property. Helen identifies and verifies Nina's identity using her original government-issued driver's licence. Helen does not seek any information about Oscar, believing that Oscar is not her client and her obligation runs only to Nina.

Has Helen satisfied her identification and verification obligations under By-Law 7.1?
opts: Yes — Helen's client is Nina, and she has satisfied her obligation by identifying and verifying Nina; she owes no obligations to Oscar, who is a third party.

No — where a client is acting as an agent or nominee for a third-party principal, By-Law 7.1 requires the lawyer to identify and verify both the client (Nina) and the third party (Oscar) on whose behalf the transaction is being conducted.
Yes — Helen is required to identify and verify Oscar only if Oscar will be attending the transaction in person.
No — Helen must obtain a statutory declaration from Oscar confirming his identity before the transaction can proceed.
expl: By-Law 7.1 imposes a dual identification and verification obligation where a client is acting on behalf of a third party — i.e., as an agent, nominee, or trustee. The rationale is anti-money laundering: allowing transactions to proceed through nominees or agents without identifying the underlying beneficial owner defeats the purpose of the client identification regime. Helen must therefore identify and verify both Nina (as the client instructing her) and Oscar (as the third-party principal whose funds will be used and who will benefit from the transaction). Failure to identify and verify Oscar is a breach of By-Law 7.1.
caseRef: LSO By-Law 7.1 (third-party verification — nominee and agent transactions)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 12 | Client Identification and Verification

text:
